title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.1 Setup
Loading data gets it into your environment. Cleaning data makes it usable. This chapter covers the systematic process of identifying and fixing data quality problems — type mismatches, inconsistent categories, duplicate records, hidden sentinel values, and the ever-present challenge of missing data. The techniques here map directly to operations you may have performed in spreadsheets, but pandas gives you the ability to apply them reproducibly, document them precisely, and scale them to datasets too large for any spreadsheet.
The central discipline of this chapter is validation: after every transformation, you check that the data changed in the way you intended and that nothing was silently lost or corrupted.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.2 Loading the Data
We will work with Boulder County voter registration data, which arrives as a pipe-delimited text file with a mix of data types, inconsistent categories, and the kinds of quality issues that appear in virtually every real-world dataset.

df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|')
df.head()

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.3 Numbers Stored as Text
One of the most common data quality problems is numeric values that pandas loaded as strings. This happens when a column contains any non-numeric values — even one — or when leading zeros need to be preserved (zip codes, ID numbers).
In a spreadsheet, you might notice this when numbers sort alphabetically (1, 10, 100, 2, 20) instead of numerically, or when formulas like SUM() return zero. In pandas, the symptom is a column with dtype: object that you expected to be numeric.

7.3.1 Forcing types at load time
The safest approach is to declare column types when you load the file, before pandas has a chance to guess:

dtype_d = {
    'RES_ZIP_CODE': str,
    'BALLOT_ZIP': str,
    'VOTER_ID': str,
    'PRECINCT': str,
}

df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|', dtype=dtype_d)



7.3.2 Converting after loading
If you need to convert a column from string to numeric after loading, use pd.to_numeric() with the errors parameter to control how conversion failures are handled:

df['YOB'] = pd.to_numeric(df['YOB'], errors='coerce')

The three options for errors each serve a different purpose. Using 'coerce' converts unparseable values to NaN, which is safe but loses information about what the original values were. Using 'ignore' leaves the column unchanged if any conversion fails, preserving data but keeping the column as strings. Using 'raise' throws an error on the first bad value, which is useful for debugging because it tells you exactly which value caused the problem.
Always check what you lost after coercing:

nulls_before = df['VOTER_ID'].isna().sum()
df['VOTER_ID_CLEAN'] = pd.to_numeric(df['VOTER_ID'], errors='coerce')
nulls_after = df['VOTER_ID_CLEAN'].isna().sum()
print(f"Conversion introduced {nulls_after - nulls_before} new nulls")

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.4 String Normalization
Text fields in real data are messy in predictable ways: inconsistent capitalization, extra whitespace, non-printing characters, and encoding artifacts. Pandas provides the .str accessor for vectorized string operations — the same operations you would apply with TRIM(), UPPER(), and CLEAN() in a spreadsheet, but applied to an entire column at once.

7.4.1 Trimming and case standardization

# Chain operations for readability
df['STREET_NAME'] = (
    df['STREET_NAME']
    .str.strip()                              # remove leading/trailing whitespace
    .str.upper()                              # standardize casing
    .str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)     # collapse internal whitespace
)



7.4.2 The validation pattern
Always compare value_counts() before and after cleaning to verify the transformation did what you intended:

# Before cleaning
print(df['RES_CITY'].value_counts())

# After cleaning
df['RES_CITY'] = df['RES_CITY'].str.strip().str.upper()
print(df['RES_CITY'].value_counts())

# Check for unintended consequences
print('New nulls:', df['RES_CITY'].isna().sum())



7.4.3 Common string artifacts
Real-world data contains predictable string problems that survive import and need active cleaning. Extra whitespace includes not just leading and trailing spaces but also non-breaking spaces (Unicode U+00A0), which are common in web-scraped data, and tabs or newlines embedded in fields. Inconsistent punctuation appears as variations like "Boulder, CO" vs. "Boulder CO" vs. "Boulder,CO", or "St. Louis" vs. "St Louis" vs. "Saint Louis". Encoding artifacts show up as garbled characters like "resumÃ©" (UTF-8 decoded as Latin-1) or "caf\xc3\xa9".

# Remove specific characters
df['VOTER_NAME'] = df['VOTER_NAME'].str.replace(',', '', regex=False)

# Replace non-breaking spaces with regular spaces
df['VOTER_NAME'] = df['VOTER_NAME'].str.replace('\xa0', ' ', regex=False)

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.5 Categorical Standardization
When a field has a controlled set of valid values — party affiliations, state abbreviations, product categories — the values in your data rarely match that set exactly. You will find abbreviations, misspellings, inconsistent capitalization, and historical codes that need to be mapped to a standard vocabulary.

7.5.1 Dictionary-based recoding with .replace()
The .replace() method takes a dictionary that maps old values to new values. Unmapped values are left unchanged:

df['PARTY'].value_counts()


party_map = {
    'DEM': 'DEM',
    'REP': 'REP',
    'UAF': 'UNA',
    'LBR': 'LBR',
    'GRN': 'GRN',
}

df['PARTY'] = df['PARTY'].str.strip().str.upper()
df['PARTY'] = df['PARTY'].replace(party_map)

# Check for unmapped values
print(df['PARTY'].value_counts())

Build the mapping dictionary by examining .value_counts() output, not by guessing. The data tells you which variants exist; your job is to decide which standard value each variant maps to.


7.5.2 .map() vs. .replace()
The .map() method is stricter: it replaces everything, and unmapped values become NaN. Use .map() when you have a controlled vocabulary and anything outside it is an error:

valid_parties = {
    'DEM': 'Democrat',
    'REP': 'Republican',
    'UNA': 'Unaffiliated',
    'LBR': 'Libertarian',
    'GRN': 'Green'
}

df['PARTY_LABEL'] = df['PARTY'].map(valid_parties)

# Find codes with no mapping
missing_labels = df[df['PARTY_LABEL'].isna() & df['PARTY'].notna()]
print(missing_labels['PARTY'].value_counts())

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.6 Sentinel Values
Sentinel values are fake entries that masquerade as real data. They are placeholders inserted to represent missing information without leaving a cell blank. Common examples include -999, 9999, or -1 in numeric fields, and "N/A", ".", "-", or "unknown" in text fields. Dates like 01/01/1900 or 12/31/9999 are date sentinels.
You can declare sentinel values at load time:

# df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', na_values=['-999', '9999', '-1', 'N/A', '.', '-', 'unknown'])

Or replace them after loading:

sentinel_to_null = {-999: np.nan, 9999: np.nan, -1: np.nan}
# df = df.replace(sentinel_to_null)

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.7 Handling Duplicates
Duplicate records are rows that share the same values on one or more key columns. They arise from data entry errors, overlapping date ranges in source files, or deliberate denormalization. The critical question is always: are these duplicates errors, or do they represent legitimately repeated observations?

7.7.1 Identifying duplicates

# Fully identical rows
full_dupes = df.duplicated()
print(f"Fully identical rows: {full_dupes.sum()}")

# Duplicates on key columns
key_dupes = df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)
print(f"Rows with duplicate VOTER_ID: {key_dupes.sum()}")



7.7.2 Understanding the pattern
Before removing duplicates, examine what varies across the repeated rows:

key_counts = df['VOTER_ID'].value_counts()
example_id = key_counts[key_counts > 1].index[0]
example_rows = df[df['VOTER_ID'] == example_id]

for col in df.columns:
    if example_rows[col].nunique() > 1:
        print(f"{col} varies: {example_rows[col].unique()}")



7.7.3 Three strategies for deduplication
Keep first or last when a more recent record supersedes an earlier one:

# df_deduped = df.sort_values('SALE_DATE').drop_duplicates(subset=['ACCOUNTNO'], keep='last')

Aggregate when duplicates represent multiple measurements:

# df_agg = df.groupby('ACCOUNTNO', as_index=False).agg({'SALE_PRICE': 'last', 'SALE_DATE': 'max'})

Flag for review when you are not sure what is right:

df['is_duplicate'] = df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)
df_clean = df[~df['is_duplicate']]
df_review = df[df['is_duplicate']].sort_values('VOTER_ID')
print(f"Clean: {len(df_clean)}, Needs review: {len(df_review)}")

Always audit totals before and after deduplication to understand the impact.

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.8 Extracting Structured Data from Text
A common problem is multiple pieces of information crammed into one field — an address field containing street, city, state, and zip code, or a name field containing first and last names.

7.8.1 Splitting on a delimiter

# Split a name column into parts
df['VOTER_NAME'].str.split(' ', expand=True).head()

The expand=True parameter returns a DataFrame instead of a Series of lists, which makes it easy to assign the parts to new columns.


7.8.2 Regular expression extraction
For more complex patterns, use .str.extract() with a named capture group regex:

# Example: extract components from a structured address
# pattern = r'^(?P<street>.*?),?\s*(?P<city>[A-Za-z\s]+),?\s*(?P<state>[A-Z]{2})\s*(?P<zip>\d{5})$'
# extracted = df['full_address'].str.extract(pattern)

Always check the extraction success rate — how many rows matched the pattern? — and investigate the failures.

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.9 Building Reusable Cleaning Functions
When you find yourself repeating the same cleaning logic across multiple columns or datasets, wrap it in a function. Functions reduce copy-paste errors, make the cleaning logic self-documenting, and enable systematic testing.

def clean_currency_column(df, col_name, new_col_name=None):
    """
    Remove currency symbols and commas, convert to numeric.

    Parameters:
        df: DataFrame
        col_name: name of column to clean
        new_col_name: name for cleaned column (default: col_name + '_clean')

    Returns:
        DataFrame with new cleaned column added.
        Prints summary of changes.
    """
    if new_col_name is None:
        new_col_name = col_name + '_clean'

    nulls_before = df[col_name].isna().sum()

    cleaned = (
        df[col_name]
        .astype(str)
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
        .str.strip()
    )

    df[new_col_name] = pd.to_numeric(cleaned, errors='coerce')

    nulls_after = df[new_col_name].isna().sum()
    nulls_introduced = nulls_after - nulls_before

    print(f"Cleaned {col_name} → {new_col_name}")
    print(f"  Nulls before: {nulls_before}")
    print(f"  Nulls after: {nulls_after}")
    print(f"  Conversion failures: {nulls_introduced} ({nulls_introduced/len(df):.2%})")

    return df

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
section: 7.10 Documentation: Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary
text:
7.10 Documentation: Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary
Every cleaning session should produce two documentation artifacts. The Known Issues Log records each problem you found, how you fixed it, and what impact the fix had — see Chapter 3 for the full format. The Data Dictionary describes every column in the cleaned dataset, including its type, valid values, and any cleaning that was applied.
These are not bureaucratic exercises. They are the only reliable way to remember your decisions when you return to the analysis later or when someone else needs to reproduce your work.

title: 7  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
7.11 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Clean the property data





Load the Boulder County residential assessment data (ar-rec-2025-residential.xlsx). Identify at least three data quality issues using the post-load checklist. Clean them, validating each transformation by comparing value_counts() or null counts before and after.






NoteSolution






arc_df = pd.read_excel('ar-rec-2025-residential.xlsx')
print('Shape:', arc_df.shape)
print('\nDtypes:')
print(arc_df.dtypes)

# Issue 1: ACCOUNTNO has trailing whitespace
arc_df['ACCOUNTNO'] = arc_df['ACCOUNTNO'].str.strip()

# Issue 2: Duplicates on ACCOUNTNO
print(f"\nDuplicate ACCOUNTNO values: {arc_df.duplicated(subset=['ACCOUNTNO']).sum()}")

# Issue 3: Check for mixed-type or sentinel values in numeric columns
print('\nSALE_PRICE sample:')
print(arc_df['SALE_PRICE'].describe())













NoteExercise 2: Write a categorical standardization function





Write a function called standardize_categorical() that takes a DataFrame, a column name, and a mapping dictionary, and returns the DataFrame with a new standardized column. The function should print a before-and-after comparison of value_counts() and report how many values were not covered by the mapping.






NoteSolution






def standardize_categorical(df, col_name, mapping_dict, new_col_name=None):
    if new_col_name is None:
        new_col_name = col_name + '_std'

    print(f"Before ({col_name}):")
    print(df[col_name].value_counts())

    df[new_col_name] = df[col_name].replace(mapping_dict)

    valid_values = set(mapping_dict.values())
    unmapped = df[~df[new_col_name].isin(valid_values)]

    print(f"\nAfter ({new_col_name}):")
    print(df[new_col_name].value_counts())
    print(f"\nUnmapped rows: {len(unmapped)} ({len(unmapped)/len(df):.1%})")

    return df

title: 8  Extending Data with pandas
text:
8.1 Setup
Loading and cleaning produce a reliable dataset. Extending transforms that dataset into something that can answer questions. This chapter covers two core operations: creating derived columns from existing data, and computing grouped summaries that collapse many rows into meaningful aggregates. These are the programmatic equivalents of writing formulas and building pivot tables in a spreadsheet — but with the power to handle millions of rows, chain operations together, and reproduce every step exactly.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)

title: 8  Extending Data with pandas
text:
8.2 Datasets
This chapter uses two datasets that illustrate different aspects of extending data.
The Lahman Baseball Database contains historical statistics for every Major League Baseball player and team. Each row in the Batting table represents one player’s performance for one team in one season. We will compute derived metrics like batting average and slugging percentage, then aggregate them across teams and decades.
The Colorado State Demography Office publishes single-year-of-age population estimates by county. Each row represents one county, one year, and one single-year age group. We will bin ages into demographic bands and compute the dependency ratio — a standard population metric.

batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')

print('Shape:', batting.shape)
print('Year range:', batting['yearID'].min(), '–', batting['yearID'].max())
batting.head()


url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/co-publicdata/sya-county.csv'
pop = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})

print('Shape:', pop.shape)
print('Year range:', pop['year'].min(), '–', pop['year'].max())
print('Datatype values:')
print(pop['datatype'].value_counts())

title: 8  Extending Data with pandas
text:
8.3 Derived Columns
A derived column is a new column computed from existing columns using arithmetic, conditional logic, or binning. The key principle is that derived columns should be transparent — anyone reading your code should be able to see exactly how the new value was computed from the originals.

8.3.1 Arithmetic: Batting Average
Batting average is hits divided by at-bats. The formula is simple, but the implementation requires a decision about what to do when at-bats is zero:

batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

print('BA stats:')
print(batting['BA'].describe())
print('Rows with NaN BA:', batting['BA'].isna().sum())

Replacing zero with NaN before dividing avoids a ZeroDivisionError and produces NaN for players with no at-bats — which is the correct semantic meaning: their batting average is undefined, not zero.


8.3.2 Multi-step formulas: Slugging Percentage and OBP
More complex metrics require computing intermediate values. Slugging percentage measures batting productivity by weighting different types of hits:

batting['TB'] = (
    batting['H']
    + 2 * batting['2B']
    + 3 * batting['3B']
    + 4 * batting['HR']
)

batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

On-base percentage measures how frequently a batter reaches base. It uses a formula with four variables in the numerator and denominator:
\[OBP = \frac{H + BB + HBP}{AB + BB + HBP + SF}\]

batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)

numerator = batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP']
denominator = batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']
batting['OBP'] = numerator / denominator

print(batting['OBP'].min(), batting['OBP'].max())

Notice the .fillna(0) calls. The HBP (hit by pitch) and SF (sacrifice fly) columns have missing values for early seasons when those statistics were not tracked. Filling with zero is a documented decision: we are treating “not tracked” as “did not happen.” This is debatable, and the Known Issues Log is where you would note this assumption.


8.3.3 Binning with pd.cut()
Binning transforms a continuous variable into categories. The Colorado population data has single-year ages (0 through 100); we want three demographic bands:

bins = [0, 18, 65, 101]
labels = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']

pop['age_band'] = pd.cut(pop['age'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)

print('Age band counts:')
print(pop['age_band'].value_counts())

The right=False parameter makes bins left-inclusive: [0, 18) means ages 0 through 17. This puts 18-year-olds in the Working Age band, which is the standard demographic convention.
Always verify that no values fell outside the bins:

print('NaN age bands:', pop['age_band'].isna().sum())



8.3.4 Integer division for decades
A quick way to create decade bins from year values:

batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

print('Decades:')
print(batting['decade'].value_counts().sort_index())

title: 8  Extending Data with pandas
text:
8.4 Grouped Summaries
The groupby() → agg() pattern is the programmatic equivalent of a spreadsheet pivot table. It splits the data into groups, applies an aggregation function to each group, and combines the results.

8.4.1 Basic groupby: Total HR per Team

latest_year = batting['yearID'].max()
yr_df = batting[batting['yearID'] == latest_year]

team_hr = (
    yr_df
    .groupby('teamID')
    .agg({'HR': 'sum'})
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('HR', ascending=False)
)

team_hr.head()



8.4.2 Filtering before aggregating
When computing averages, filter to meaningful subsets first. A player with 2 at-bats and 1 hit has a .500 batting average, but that tells you nothing useful:

qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100]

decade_ba = (
    qualified
    .groupby('decade')['BA']
    .agg('mean')
)

decade_ba.plot(title='Mean BA by Decade (players with ≥ 100 AB)')



8.4.3 The dependency ratio: a real-world grouped summary
The dependency ratio — the number of children and seniors per working-age adult — is a standard demographic metric that requires grouped aggregation to compute:

estimates = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
co_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020]

band_summary = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band']).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index()

# Validate: grouped total should match original
print(f'Grouped total:  {band_summary["pop"].sum():,.0f}')
print(f'Original total: {co_2020["totalpopulation"].sum():,.0f}')

title: 8  Extending Data with pandas
text:
8.5 Three Tools, One Job
Pandas offers three tools for creating cross-tabular summaries, and understanding when to use each saves time and prevents errors.








Tool
Aggregates?
Use when




groupby().agg()
Yes
You need flexibility, named aggregations, or long format for further computation


pivot_table()
Yes
You want aggregation and reshaping in one step (closest to an Excel pivot)


pivot()
No
Data is already one-row-per-key; you just need to reshape




8.5.1 groupby().agg() — long format output

modern = batting[(batting['yearID'] >= 1990) & (batting['yearID'] <= 2022)].copy()

hr_long = (
    modern
    .groupby(['teamID', 'decade'])['HR']
    .agg('sum')
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={'HR': 'total_HR'})
)

print('Shape:', hr_long.shape, '← one row per (team, decade)')
hr_long.head()



8.5.2 pivot_table() — wide format in one step

hr_wide_pt = modern.pivot_table(
    index='teamID',
    columns='decade',
    values='HR',
    aggfunc='sum',
    fill_value=0
)

print('Shape:', hr_wide_pt.shape, '← one row per team, one column per decade')
hr_wide_pt.head()



8.5.3 pivot() — reshape only, no aggregation
pivot() requires every (index, columns) combination to appear exactly once in the data. It will raise a ValueError if there are duplicates, because it cannot aggregate:

# This WORKS because hr_long has one row per (team, decade)
hr_wide_pv = hr_long.pivot(
    index='teamID',
    columns='decade',
    values='total_HR'
)

print('Shape:', hr_wide_pv.shape)



8.5.4 Verifying all three agree

total_long = hr_long['total_HR'].sum()
total_pt = hr_wide_pt.values.sum()
total_pv = hr_wide_pv.fillna(0).values.sum()

print(f'groupby().agg() total HR: {total_long:,}')
print(f'pivot_table()   total HR: {total_pt:,}')
print(f'pivot()         total HR: {total_pv:,}')
print(f'All match: {total_long == total_pt == total_pv}')

title: 8  Extending Data with pandas
text:
8.6 agg() vs. transform()
Both operate on groups, but they produce fundamentally different outputs. The agg() method produces one row per group — the output is smaller than the input. The transform() method broadcasts the group-level result back to every row in the original DataFrame — the output has the same number of rows as the input.

# agg() — summary table
team_totals = (
    batting[batting['yearID'] == 2022]
    .groupby('teamID')['HR']
    .agg('sum')
    .reset_index()
)
print('agg() shape:', team_totals.shape, '(one row per team)')


# transform() — adds group stat to every row
batting['team_total_HR'] = (
    batting
    .groupby(['yearID', 'teamID'])['HR']
    .transform('sum')
)

batting['hr_share'] = batting['HR'] / batting['team_total_HR'].replace(0, float('nan'))

Use transform() when you want to compute something like “what percentage of team home runs did each player contribute?” — a question that requires both the individual value and the group total on the same row.

title: 8  Extending Data with pandas
text:
8.7 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Compute OPS and identify top performers





OPS is On-base Plus Slugging: OPS = OBP + SLG. Add an OPS column to the batting DataFrame. Filter to players with at least 400 at-bats in a single season, and find the top 10 OPS seasons of all time.






NoteSolution






batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']

top_ops = (
    batting[batting['AB'] >= 400]
    .sort_values('OPS', ascending=False)
    [['playerID', 'yearID', 'teamID', 'AB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS']]
    .head(10)
)
top_ops













NoteExercise 2: Compute the dependency ratio for all counties in 2020





Using the Colorado population data, filter to Estimate rows for 2020, bin ages into the three standard bands, and compute the dependency ratio for each county. Which county has the highest dependency ratio? The lowest?






NoteSolution






co_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020].copy()

wide = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index().pivot_table(
    index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop', aggfunc='sum'
).reset_index()
wide.columns.name = None

wide['dep_ratio'] = (
    (wide['Children (0-17)'] + wide['Seniors (65+)'])
    / wide['Working Age (18-64)']
)

print('Highest:', wide.sort_values('dep_ratio', ascending=False).head(3)[['county', 'dep_ratio']].to_string())
print('\nLowest:', wide.sort_values('dep_ratio').head(3)[['county', 'dep_ratio']].to_string())













NoteExercise 3: Compare pivot_table() and groupby() → pivot() paths





Using the dependency ratio data from Exercise 2, implement the same result using the two-step groupby().agg() → pivot() path. Verify that both approaches produce identical results using pd.testing.assert_frame_equal().






NoteSolution






# Path A: pivot_table (one step)
wide_pt = co_2020.pivot_table(
    index='county', columns='age_band', values='totalpopulation', aggfunc='sum'
)

# Path B: groupby → pivot (two steps)
long_agg = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index()

wide_pv = long_agg.pivot(index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop')

# Verify
pd.testing.assert_frame_equal(
    wide_pt.sort_index(), wide_pv.sort_index(), check_names=False
)
print('Both paths produce identical DataFrames.')

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.1 Setup
Most analysis questions require data from multiple sources, and those sources rarely arrive in the shape you need. This chapter covers the operations that change the structure of your data: melt() to go from wide to long, pivot() and pivot_table() to go from long to wide, concat() to stack DataFrames vertically, and merge() to combine tables by matching on shared keys. Each operation carries risks — silent row inflation, dropped records, introduced nulls — that you must audit after every transformation.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.2 Datasets
This chapter continues with the Lahman Baseball Database and Colorado population data from Chapter 8, and adds two new Lahman tables — Salaries and People — to demonstrate multi-table merging.

batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')

# Derived columns from previous chapter
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF'])
    .replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

print('Batting shape:', batting.shape)


salaries = pd.read_csv('Salaries.csv')
print('Salaries shape:', salaries.shape)
print('Year range:', salaries['yearID'].min(), '–', salaries['yearID'].max())


people = pd.read_csv('People.csv')
print('People shape:', people.shape)
people[['playerID', 'nameFirst', 'nameLast', 'birthYear', 'birthState']].head()

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.3 Wide vs. Long Format
The same data can be represented in two fundamentally different shapes.
Wide format has one row per entity and multiple columns for repeated measurements. A table of HR by decade with teams as rows and decades as columns is wide format.
Long format (also called “tidy” format) has one row per observation. The same HR data in long format has one row per (team, decade) combination, with a single column for the HR value.
Neither format is inherently better. Wide format is easier for humans to read as a cross-tab. Long format is easier for computation — groupby(), merge(), and plotting functions generally expect long format. The skill is knowing how to convert between the two.

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.4 melt(): Wide to Long
The melt() function unpivots a wide DataFrame into long format. You specify which columns stay as identifiers (id_vars) and which columns get “melted” into a single key-value pair (value_vars).

# Build a wide pivot first
c0 = batting['yearID'] >= 1900
c1 = batting['yearID'] < 2020
batting_modern = batting[c0 & c1].copy()

hr_wide = batting_modern.pivot_table(
    index='decade',
    columns='teamID',
    values='HR',
    aggfunc='sum'
).reset_index()
hr_wide.columns.name = None

print('Wide shape:', hr_wide.shape)


team_cols = [c for c in hr_wide.columns if c != 'decade']

hr_long = hr_wide.melt(
    id_vars=['decade'],
    value_vars=team_cols,
    var_name='teamID',
    value_name='total_HR'
)

print('Long shape:', hr_long.shape)
print('Expected rows:', hr_wide.shape[0], '×', len(team_cols), '=', hr_wide.shape[0] * len(team_cols))

After melting, some rows will contain NaN — a team that did not exist in a given decade. Always drop or flag these:

hr_clean = hr_long.dropna(subset=['total_HR'])
print('After dropping NaN:', hr_clean.shape)

The power of long format is that it enables further computation that would be awkward in wide format:

# League-wide mean HR per team by decade — easy in long, awkward in wide
league_avg = (
    hr_clean
    .groupby('decade')['total_HR']
    .agg(n_teams='count', mean_HR='mean', max_HR='max')
    .reset_index()
)

league_avg.set_index('decade')['mean_HR'].plot(title='Mean Team HR by Decade')

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.5 concat(): Stacking DataFrames
The concat() function appends rows from multiple DataFrames into one. This is the programmatic equivalent of copy-pasting rows across Excel sheets. Use it when you have multiple files with the same columns — annual exports, regional splits, or train/test splits.

dead_ball = batting[batting['yearID'] < 1920].copy()
live_ball = batting[(batting['yearID'] >= 1920) & (batting['yearID'] < 1970)].copy()
modern = batting[batting['yearID'] >= 1970].copy()

frames = [dead_ball, live_ball, modern]
all_eras = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)

print('Shape after concat:', all_eras.shape)
print('Row count matches original?', all_eras.shape[0] == batting.shape[0])







WarningThe validation pattern after concat()



Always check three things after concatenating: (1) row count equals the sum of input row counts, (2) no new NaN columns appeared (column name mismatches create them silently), and (3) no unexpected duplicates from overlapping date ranges.



9.5.1 Tracking provenance with keys
When files come from different sources, use the keys parameter to record which file each row came from:

era_labels = ['Dead Ball (<1920)', 'Live Ball (1920-1969)', 'Modern (1970+)']

all_eras_labeled = pd.concat(
    frames,
    keys=era_labels,
    ignore_index=False
).reset_index(level=0).rename(columns={'level_0': 'era'})

print(all_eras_labeled['era'].value_counts())

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.6 merge(): Combining Tables by Key
Merging is the most powerful and most dangerous reshaping operation. It combines two DataFrames by matching rows on one or more shared key columns — the programmatic equivalent of VLOOKUP in a spreadsheet, but with support for many-to-many relationships, multiple keys, and all join types.

9.6.1 Step 1: Inspect keys before merging
Before any merge, verify that your keys have the same dtype in both tables and understand the cardinality — is the key unique in each table, or can it repeat?

print('Batting playerID dtype:', batting['playerID'].dtype)
print('Salaries playerID dtype:', salaries['playerID'].dtype)
print()
print('Batting: max rows per playerID+yearID:',
      batting.groupby(['playerID', 'yearID']).size().max(),
      '(>1 means multi-stint seasons)')
print('Salaries: max rows per playerID+yearID:',
      salaries.groupby(['playerID', 'yearID']).size().max())

The batting table has players with multiple rows per season — players traded mid-year get one row per team. This means a merge on playerID + yearID alone could produce multiple matches. The solution is to aggregate batting to one row per player-season first:

batting_season = (
    batting
    .groupby(['playerID', 'yearID'])
    .agg(
        AB=('AB', 'sum'), H=('H', 'sum'), TB=('TB', 'sum'),
        BB=('BB', 'sum'), HBP=('HBP', 'sum'), SF=('SF', 'sum'),
        HR=('HR', 'sum'), G=('G', 'sum')
    )
    .reset_index()
)

# Recompute rates on aggregated totals
batting_season['BA'] = batting_season['H'] / batting_season['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting_season['SLG'] = batting_season['TB'] / batting_season['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting_season['OPS'] = batting_season['BA'] + batting_season['SLG']  # simplified

print('Aggregated shape:', batting_season.shape, '(one row per player-season)')



9.6.2 Step 2: Merge with indicator=True
The indicator=True parameter adds a _merge column that shows whether each row matched in both tables, only in the left, or only in the right:

bat_sal = pd.merge(
    left=batting_season,
    right=salaries[['playerID', 'yearID', 'salary']],
    on=['playerID', 'yearID'],
    how='left',
    indicator=True
)

print('Before merge:', batting_season.shape[0], 'rows')
print('After merge:', bat_sal.shape[0], 'rows')
print()
print('Merge audit:')
print(bat_sal['_merge'].value_counts())
print(f'\nMatch rate: {(bat_sal["_merge"] == "both").mean():.1%}')



9.6.3 Step 3: Understand unmatched rows

unmatched = bat_sal[bat_sal['_merge'] == 'left_only']
print('Unmatched year range:', unmatched['yearID'].min(), '–', unmatched['yearID'].max())

The Lahman salary data begins in 1985. All seasons before that have no salary information — this is a structural gap in the data, not an error in the merge. Understanding why rows do not match is as important as knowing that they do not match.


9.6.4 Adding biographical data

bio_cols = ['playerID', 'nameFirst', 'nameLast', 'birthYear', 'birthState', 'birthCountry']
people_slim = people[bio_cols].copy()

bat_sal_bio = pd.merge(bat_sal, people_slim, on='playerID', how='left')

print('Shape after adding bio:', bat_sal_bio.shape)
print('Missing nameFirst:', bat_sal_bio['nameFirst'].isna().sum())

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.7 Applied Analysis: Salary and Performance
With a merged dataset, you can answer questions that required data from multiple tables.

# Filter to salary era with meaningful at-bats
qualified = bat_sal_bio[
    (bat_sal_bio['yearID'] >= 1985) &
    (bat_sal_bio['AB'] >= 200) &
    (bat_sal_bio['salary'].notna())
].copy()

qualified['sal_decile'] = pd.qcut(
    qualified['salary'], q=10,
    labels=['D1 (Lowest)', 'D2', 'D3', 'D4', 'D5',
            'D6', 'D7', 'D8', 'D9', 'D10 (Highest)']
)

perf_by_salary = (
    qualified
    .groupby('sal_decile', observed=True)[['BA', 'SLG']]
    .agg('mean')
    .round(3)
)

print('Mean batting stats by salary decile:')
perf_by_salary

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
section: 9.8 MultiIndex: When Groups Become the Index
text:
9.8 MultiIndex: When Groups Become the Index
After groupby().agg() on multiple columns, pandas returns a MultiIndex — a hierarchical index that can be confusing if you are not expecting it. The most common solution is to call .reset_index() immediately to flatten it back to regular columns:

# Without reset_index — produces a MultiIndex
team_stats = (
    batting_modern
    .groupby(['decade', 'teamID'])[['HR', 'BA']]
    .agg(['mean', 'sum'])
)

print('MultiIndex columns:', team_stats.columns[:4])


# Flatten with reset_index and column renaming
team_stats.columns = ['_'.join(col) for col in team_stats.columns]
team_stats = team_stats.reset_index()
team_stats.head()

title: 9  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
9.9 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Melt and validate





Take the wide dependency ratio table from Chapter 8 (counties as rows, age bands as columns). Melt it back to long format. Verify that the population totals match between the wide and long versions.






NoteSolution






age_band_cols = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']

long_2020 = wide_2020.melt(
    id_vars=['county'],
    value_vars=age_band_cols,
    var_name='age_band',
    value_name='pop'
)

print('Wide sum:', wide_2020[age_band_cols].sum().sum())
print('Long sum:', long_2020['pop'].sum())

Merge the Lahman Teams.csv table onto the batting data using teamID and yearID as keys. Use indicator=True and report the match rate. How many batting rows have no corresponding team record?






NoteSolution






teams = pd.read_csv('Teams.csv')

bat_team = pd.merge(
    batting,
    teams[['teamID', 'yearID', 'name', 'W', 'L']],
    on=['teamID', 'yearID'],

    indicator=True

print(bat_team['_merge'].value_counts())
print(f'\nMatch rate: {(bat_team["_merge"] == "both").mean():.1%}')

Using the merged bat_sal_bio DataFrame, find all player-seasons where birthState == 'CO' and AB >= 100. Who are the top 10 Colorado-born players by career OPS?

title: 10  Loading Data with SQL
text:
10.1 Terminal Orientation
This chapter marks the beginning of a new tool — SQL and the SQLite database engine — applied to the same Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape workflow you already know from pandas. Many of the concepts are identical: you are still loading data from files, inspecting it for problems, checking types and counts, and verifying that nothing went wrong. What changes is the interface: instead of calling Python methods on a DataFrame, you write declarative queries that describe what you want, and the database engine figures out how to get it.
SQL (Structured Query Language) has been the standard language for working with databases since the 1970s. You will encounter it in virtually every data-related career. SQLite is a lightweight database engine that stores an entire database in a single file on disk — no server, no installation wizard, no network configuration. This makes it ideal for learning SQL and for working with datasets that fit on a single machine.
The workflow in this chapter moves through four stages: orienting to the terminal, creating a database and schema in the SQLite shell, connecting to the database from Python, and running diagnostic queries with pd.read_sql_query().
Before touching SQLite, you need three terminal commands. If you completed Chapter 1, these are review.

title: 10  Loading Data with SQL
text:
Command (Mac/Linux)
Command (Windows)
Purpose




pwd
cd (no arguments)
Print the current directory


ls
dir
List files in the current directory


cd folder
cd folder
Move into a folder




10.1.1 Checking SQLite installation
sqlite3 --version
If you see a version number (e.g., 3.43.2), SQLite is installed and on your PATH. If you get command not found, see Chapter 4 for installation instructions.

title: 10  Loading Data with SQL
text:
10.2 The SQLite Shell
The SQLite shell (sqlite3) is a command-line interface for interacting with a database directly. Think of it as a text-based spreadsheet viewer that also lets you define tables and run queries.

10.2.1 Dot-commands vs. SQL statements
The SQLite shell has two distinct types of commands. Dot-commands start with a period, do not end with a semicolon, and control the shell itself (.tables, .schema, .import, .quit). SQL statements are standard SQL, always end with a semicolon, and operate on data (SELECT ...;, CREATE TABLE ...;).
A SQL statement without a semicolon will sit and wait — SQLite thinks you are still typing. Adding a semicolon after a dot-command will produce an error. Getting these conventions straight early saves considerable frustration.


10.2.2 Step 1: Create the database file
Running sqlite3 cannabis.db either opens an existing file or creates a new one:
sqlite3 cannabis.db ".tables"
The file is created on disk when you first write data to it.


10.2.3 Step 2: Prepare the CSV
SQLite’s .import command expects a clean, rectangular CSV with exactly one header row and no aggregate rows. Real-world files rarely meet this requirement out of the box. The standard approach is to clean the file in pandas first — using the same parameters from Chapter 6 — and export a clean CSV for SQLite to consume.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df_raw = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

# Rename to clean, SQL-friendly column names
df_raw.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical_sales', 'retail_sales']

# Strip currency formatting and cast to nullable integer
for col in ['medical_sales', 'retail_sales']:
    df_raw[col] = (
        df_raw[col]
        .str.strip()
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
        .pipe(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
        .astype('Int64')
    )

# Remove aggregate Total rows
df_raw = df_raw[df_raw['county'].str.strip() != 'Total']

# Export
df_raw.to_csv('cannabis_clean.csv', index=False)
print(f"Exported {len(df_raw):,} rows to cannabis_clean.csv")







TipSQL naming rules



Use lowercase letters, underscores, and no spaces or special characters for column names. The names in the CSV must exactly match the names in the CREATE TABLE schema you will define next. A mismatch causes the header row to be inserted as a data row — one of the most common SQLite loading errors.




10.2.4 Step 3: Define the schema
Before loading data, you declare the table structure — column names and their storage types:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cannabis_sales (
    month INTEGER,
    year INTEGER,
    county TEXT,
    medical_sales INTEGER,
    retail_sales INTEGER
);
SQLite has five storage classes: INTEGER, REAL, TEXT, BLOB, and NULL. For most work, you will use INTEGER, REAL, and TEXT.


10.2.5 Step 4: Load the CSV
The .import command reads a delimited file and inserts rows into a table. Always set .mode csv and .headers on before importing:
sqlite3 cannabis.db << 'EOF'
.mode csv
.headers on
.import cannabis_clean.csv cannabis_sales
EOF






WarningThe heredoc syntax



The << 'EOF' syntax sends multiple lines of input to SQLite through stdin. This is necessary because dot-commands cannot be passed as quoted string arguments on the command line. If you are on Windows, use the Python-side approach described in the next section instead.




10.2.6 Step 5: Post-load diagnostics
Run three checks immediately after loading:
# 1. Row count
sqlite3 cannabis.db "SELECT COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM cannabis_sales;"

# 2. First rows
sqlite3 -column -header cannabis.db "SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 5;"

# 3. Check for accidentally imported header row
sqlite3 cannabis.db "SELECT COUNT(*) AS header_rows FROM cannabis_sales WHERE county = 'county';"
If the header row count is greater than zero, delete it:
DELETE FROM cannabis_sales WHERE county = 'county';

title: 10  Loading Data with SQL
text:
10.3 Connecting from Python
The terminal workflow above is useful for setup and debugging, but your analysis work will happen in Jupyter notebooks using Python’s built-in sqlite3 module and pandas’ pd.read_sql_query().

import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect('cannabis.db')
print("Connection established:", conn)







Warning



If the path is wrong, sqlite3.connect() will silently create a new empty file rather than raising an error. If your queries return “no such table,” check that you are pointing at the right file.



10.3.1 Low-level access with a cursor

cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM cannabis_sales;")
result = cursor.fetchone()
print("Row count:", result[0])



10.3.2 The preferred workflow: pd.read_sql_query()
This function sends a SQL query and returns a DataFrame — exactly like pd.read_csv(), but reading from a database instead of a file:

df = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 10;", conn)
df

The result is a real pandas DataFrame. All the methods you learned in previous chapters — .shape, .dtypes, .describe(), .value_counts() — work exactly the same way.

title: 10  Loading Data with SQL
text:
10.4 SQL Syntax Fundamentals
Every query you write follows a standard clause structure. The clauses must appear in this order:
SELECT   county, year, retail_sales      -- 1. Which columns to return
FROM     cannabis_sales                  -- 2. Which table to read from
WHERE    year = 2023                     -- 3. Which rows to keep (filter)
GROUP BY county                          -- 4. How to collapse rows into groups
HAVING   SUM(retail_sales) > 1000000     -- 5. Filter on group-level results
ORDER BY retail_sales DESC               -- 6. How to sort the output
LIMIT    10;                             -- 7. Maximum rows to return
Not every clause is required. A minimal query needs only SELECT and FROM:
SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales;

10.4.1 Keywords and case
SQL is case-insensitive for keywords — SELECT, select, and Select all work. The convention is to capitalize SQL keywords and use lowercase for table names, column names, and aliases. This helps your eye parse the clause structure quickly.


10.4.2 Literals
When you write a value directly in a query, always use single quotes for text strings ('Boulder'), no quotes for numbers (2023), and NULL for missing values. Double quotes in SQLite are for identifiers (column names), not string values.


10.4.3 The NULL gotcha
NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL, not TRUE. You cannot use = to test for missing values. Always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.

title: 10  Loading Data with SQL
text:
10.5 Exploratory Queries
With the data loaded and a Python connection established, apply the post-load checklist using SQL:

# Shape equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM cannabis_sales;", conn)


# Head equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 5;", conn)


# Value counts equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n_months
    FROM cannabis_sales
    GROUP BY county
    ORDER BY n_months DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)


# Describe equivalent for a numeric column
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        MIN(retail_sales) AS min_val,
        MAX(retail_sales) AS max_val,
        AVG(retail_sales) AS mean_val,
        COUNT(*) AS n_rows,
        SUM(CASE WHEN retail_sales IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS n_nulls
    FROM cannabis_sales;
""", conn)

Clean the Colorado population data in pandas, export it as a CSV, define a schema for a population table, and load it into the same database. Run the three post-load diagnostic checks.

pop = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate']

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS population (
    countyfips TEXT,
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,
    age INTEGER,
    malepopulation INTEGER,
    femalepopulation INTEGER,
    totalpopulation INTEGER,
    datatype TEXT

Then import using .mode csv, .headers on, and .import population_clean.csv population.












NoteExercise 2: Diagnostic queries





Write SQL queries to answer: (a) How many distinct counties are in the cannabis_sales table? (b) What is the year range? (c) How many rows have NULL retail_sales? (d) Which county has the most months of data?






NoteSolution






queries = [
    "SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT county) AS n_counties FROM cannabis_sales;",
    "SELECT MIN(year) AS min_year, MAX(year) AS max_year FROM cannabis_sales;",
    "SELECT COUNT(*) AS null_retail FROM cannabis_sales WHERE retail_sales IS NULL;",
    """SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n_months FROM cannabis_sales
       GROUP BY county ORDER BY n_months DESC LIMIT 5;"""
]

for q in queries:
    print(pd.read_sql_query(q, conn))
    print()

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.1 The Diagnostic Framework
Cleaning data in SQL follows the same logic you learned in pandas — identify missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, and invalid ranges — but uses a different set of tools. SQL excels at diagnostic queries: you can characterize problems across an entire table with a single statement, and the declarative syntax makes your logic explicit and auditable. This chapter applies a structured diagnostic framework to three real Colorado government datasets, loads them into a SQLite database, and demonstrates a critical lesson about silent join failures caused by inconsistent key formatting across sources.
Every cleaning session in this chapter follows the same five stages:
Within the Inspect stage, we run four standard diagnostics, one per category of data quality issue:
This is the same INSPECT → TRANSFORM → VALIDATE → DOCUMENT workflow from the pandas chapters, specialized for SQL.

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
Inspect → Clean → Design Schema → Load → Validate


Missingness — which columns have nulls, and how many?
Duplicates — are there repeated rows on the expected key columns?
Inconsistent categories — do text columns have variant spellings, extra whitespace, or unexpected values?
Invalid ranges — are numeric and date columns within plausible bounds?

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.2 Setup

import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)

conn = sqlite3.connect('colorado_public_health.db')
cursor = conn.cursor()

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.3 Datasets
This chapter works with three Colorado government datasets that will be joined together in Chapter 13.








File
Source
Description




cdc_deaths.csv
CDC WONDER
Colorado mortality counts by county, year, age group, and ICD-10 chapter


cbi_crime.csv
Colorado Bureau of Investigation
Reported offenses by jurisdiction, date, and offense type


sya-county.csv
State Demography Office
Single-year-of-age population estimates by county and year

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.4 Part 1: CDC Mortality Data

11.4.1 1.1 Inspect
Load the raw file in pandas and run each diagnostic.

deaths_raw = pd.read_csv('cdc_deaths.csv')
print('Shape:', deaths_raw.shape)
deaths_raw.head()


11.4.1.1 Missingness

print(deaths_raw.isna().sum())



11.4.1.2 Duplicates

key_cols = ['County', 'Year', 'Age Group', 'ICD Chapter']
print('Duplicates on key:', deaths_raw.duplicated(subset=key_cols).sum())



11.4.1.3 Inconsistent categories

print(deaths_raw['County'].value_counts().head(10))

Pay attention to the format of county names. Does this dataset use 'Adams', 'Adams County', or 'Adams County, CO'? This will matter critically when we join tables later.


11.4.1.4 Invalid ranges

print('Year range:', deaths_raw['Year'].min(), '–', deaths_raw['Year'].max())
print('Deaths range:', deaths_raw['Deaths'].min(), '–', deaths_raw['Deaths'].max())




11.4.2 1.2 Clean
Apply cleaning operations in pandas based on what the diagnostics revealed. Strip whitespace from text columns, standardize county names, and handle any sentinel values (CDC WONDER uses “Suppressed” and “Unreliable” as data quality flags).

deaths_clean = deaths_raw.copy()

# Strip whitespace from all text columns
for col in deaths_clean.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    deaths_clean[col] = deaths_clean[col].str.strip()



11.4.3 1.3 Design Schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS deaths (
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,
    age_group TEXT,
    icd_chapter TEXT,
    deaths INTEGER
);


11.4.4 1.4 Load

deaths_clean.to_csv('deaths_clean.csv', index=False)



11.4.5 1.5 Validate
After loading into SQLite, run diagnostic queries to confirm the import matches the pandas source:

pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM deaths;", conn)


pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM deaths
    GROUP BY county
    ORDER BY n DESC
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

Cross-check the SQL count against the pandas count — they should match exactly.

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.5 Part 2: CBI Crime Data
The same five-stage process applies. The crime data introduces additional challenges: jurisdiction names that do not match county names, nullable columns with complex patterns, and timestamp fields that require careful parsing.

crime_raw = pd.read_csv('cbi_crime.csv')
print('Shape:', crime_raw.shape)
crime_raw.head()

Run all four diagnostics (missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, invalid ranges), clean the data, design a schema, load it, and validate the load.

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.6 Part 3: Population Estimates

pop_raw = pd.read_csv('sya-county.csv', skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
pop_raw = pop_raw[pop_raw['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
print('Shape:', pop_raw.shape)
pop_raw.head()

The population data has its own quirks: a header row that needs to be skipped, a datatype column that mixes estimates and forecasts, and county names in yet another format.

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.7 Part 4: The Silent Join Failure
This is the most important section of the chapter. After loading all three tables, we discover that county names are formatted differently in each source:



Table
Example county name




deaths
Adams County, CO


crime
Adams County


population
Adams



What happens when you try to join these tables on county name? Nothing matches. The join produces zero rows — silently, with no error message. This is a silent join failure, and it is one of the most dangerous problems in data work because it looks like the query succeeded (it ran without errors) but the result is empty or incomplete.

11.7.1 Demonstrating the failure

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT d.county AS death_county, p.county AS pop_county, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM deaths d
    JOIN population p ON d.county = p.county
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

If this returns zero rows, the county formats do not match.


11.7.2 Diagnosing the mismatch

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT county FROM deaths ORDER BY county LIMIT 10;
""", conn)


pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT county FROM population ORDER BY county LIMIT 10;
""", conn)



11.7.3 Fixing the mismatch
The fix is to standardize county names to a common format before loading, or to use SQL string functions to normalize them at query time. The REPLACE() and TRIM() functions in SQLite can help, but the cleanest approach is to fix the data in pandas before loading:

# Standardize all county names to bare names: 'Adams'
# From 'Adams County, CO' → remove ', CO' then remove ' County'
# From 'Adams County' → remove ' County'
# From 'Adams' → already correct



11.7.4 The lesson
County name mismatches across sources are not a quirk of these particular datasets — they are a universal pattern. Any time you join data from different sources on a text key, you must verify that the key values actually match before running the join. The diagnostic pattern is: list the distinct values from each table, compare them, and fix any mismatches before joining.

title: 11  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
11.8 SQL Functions for Cleaning
Several SQL functions are useful for cleaning operations:








Function
Purpose
Example




TRIM(col)
Remove leading/trailing whitespace
SELECT TRIM(county) FROM deaths;


UPPER(col)
Convert to uppercase
SELECT UPPER(county) FROM deaths;


LOWER(col)
Convert to lowercase
SELECT LOWER(county) FROM deaths;


REPLACE(col, old, new)
Replace substring
SELECT REPLACE(county, ' County', '') FROM deaths;


CAST(col AS type)
Change data type
SELECT CAST(year AS INTEGER) FROM deaths;


COALESCE(col, default)
Replace NULL with default
SELECT COALESCE(deaths, 0) FROM deaths;


CASE WHEN
Conditional logic
See below




11.8.1 CASE WHEN for conditional cleaning
SELECT
    county,
    CASE
        WHEN deaths IS NULL THEN 'Missing'
        WHEN deaths = 0 THEN 'Zero'
        WHEN deaths < 10 THEN 'Suppressed range'
        ELSE 'Normal'
    END AS death_status
FROM deaths;

title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
text:
12.1 Setup
Loading and cleaning produce reliable tables. Extending transforms those tables into answers. This chapter introduces the SQL tools for computing metrics and testing analytical claims: GROUP BY with aggregate functions, subqueries that embed one query inside another, and Common Table Expressions (CTEs) that name intermediate results for clarity and reuse. Together, these tools let you write complex analytical pipelines entirely in SQL — pipelines that are readable, auditable, and reproducible.
The dataset for this chapter is the Boulder County Assessor’s buildings file, which contains one row per improvement section recorded for each parcel. It is a rich, messy, real-world dataset with dozens of columns describing building characteristics — year built, square footage, quality grade, heating system, roof type, and more.
import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.2f}'.format)
# Load the CSV, clean, and write to a local SQLite database
buildings_raw = pd.read_csv('buildings.csv')

# Strip trailing whitespace from text columns
for col in buildings_raw.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    buildings_raw[col] = buildings_raw[col].str.strip()

# Write to database
conn = sqlite3.connect('boulder_buildings.db')
buildings_raw.to_sql('buildings', conn, if_exists='replace', index=False)

print(f"Loaded {len(buildings_raw):,} rows into buildings table")

title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
section: 12.2 Part 1: GROUP BY and Aggregate Functions
text:
12.2 Part 1: GROUP BY and Aggregate Functions

12.2.1 What GROUP BY does
GROUP BY collapses rows that share the same value(s) in one or more columns into a single summary row. It is the SQL equivalent of pandas’ groupby().agg() and the conceptual equivalent of a spreadsheet pivot table.
The key rule: every column in your SELECT must be either listed in GROUP BY or wrapped in an aggregate function. You cannot select a raw column alongside grouped aggregates — SQL would not know which row’s value to display.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        quality_grade,
        COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
        AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft,
        MIN(year_built) AS oldest,
        MAX(year_built) AS newest
    FROM buildings
    WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    ORDER BY n_buildings DESC;
""", conn)



12.2.2 SQLite aggregate functions








Function
Purpose
Notes




COUNT(*)
Number of rows in group
Counts all rows, including NULLs


COUNT(col)
Number of non-NULL values
Skips NULLs — use to measure completeness


SUM(col)
Sum of values
Returns NULL if all values are NULL


TOTAL(col)
Sum of values
Returns 0.0 if all values are NULL (safer)


AVG(col)
Mean of non-NULL values
Ignores NULLs — denominator is count of non-NULL


MIN(col) / MAX(col)
Extremes
Works on text (alphabetical) and numbers


GROUP_CONCAT(col)
Concatenate values into a string
Useful for inventory lists



The distinction between COUNT(*) and COUNT(col) is critical. COUNT(*) counts all rows regardless of NULL values. COUNT(col) counts only rows where col is not NULL. Comparing the two tells you the completeness rate of a column.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
        COUNT(year_built) AS non_null_year,
        COUNT(*) - COUNT(year_built) AS null_year,
        ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(year_built) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_complete
    FROM buildings;
""", conn)



12.2.3 HAVING vs. WHERE
Both filter rows, but they operate at different stages of query execution. WHERE filters individual rows before grouping. HAVING filters groups after aggregation.

# WHERE: filter rows before grouping
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT quality_grade, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built >= 2000
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    ORDER BY n DESC;
""", conn)


# HAVING: filter groups after aggregation
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT quality_grade, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM buildings
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    HAVING n >= 100
    ORDER BY n DESC;
""", conn)

A useful mnemonic: WHERE answers “which rows go into the groups?” while HAVING answers “which groups appear in the output?”


12.2.4 CASE WHEN inside aggregation
CASE WHEN expressions inside aggregate functions enable conditional counting — the SQL equivalent of COUNTIF in a spreadsheet. This is one of the most powerful patterns in SQL.

# Cross-tab: count buildings by decade and class
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        (year_built / 10) * 10 AS decade,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class = 'R' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS residential,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class = 'C' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS commercial,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class NOT IN ('R', 'C') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS other,
        COUNT(*) AS total
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY decade
    ORDER BY decade;
""", conn)

title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
text:
12.3 Part 1 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Construction era distribution





Write a query that groups buildings into construction eras — pre-1950, 1950–1979, 1980–1999, 2000–present — and counts the number of buildings in each era. Use CASE WHEN to create the era labels.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        CASE
            WHEN year_built < 1950 THEN 'Pre-1950'
            WHEN year_built < 1980 THEN '1950-1979'
            WHEN year_built < 2000 THEN '1980-1999'
            ELSE '2000-present'
        END AS era,
        COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
        ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY era
    ORDER BY MIN(year_built);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 2: Heating system prevalence





Write a query that shows the top 10 heating system types by count, along with the average square footage and average year built for buildings with each heating type. Exclude rows where the heating system is NULL.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        heating_type,
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft,
        ROUND(AVG(year_built), 0) AS avg_year
    FROM buildings
    WHERE heating_type IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY heating_type
    ORDER BY n DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
text:
12.4 Part 2: Subqueries
A subquery is a SELECT statement nested inside another query. It lets you compute a value or a set of values dynamically and use the result in a filter, comparison, or derived table — without hardcoding anything.

12.4.1 Scalar subquery: comparing each row to a reference value
A scalar subquery returns a single value. You can use it anywhere a literal value would go:

# Find buildings larger than the overall average
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT strap, year_built, above_ground_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE above_ground_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings)
    ORDER BY above_ground_sqft DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

The subquery (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings) computes the mean once, and the outer query uses it to filter rows. This is more robust than hardcoding the average — if the data changes, the query automatically adapts.


12.4.2 WHERE IN: filtering against a dynamically computed set

# Find buildings in quality grades that have above-average mean square footage
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT strap, quality_grade, above_ground_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE quality_grade IN (
        SELECT quality_grade
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
        HAVING AVG(above_ground_sqft) > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings)
    )
    ORDER BY above_ground_sqft DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)



12.4.3 Derived table: wrapping aggregate results
A subquery in the FROM clause creates a temporary table (called a derived table) that the outer query can filter, join, or aggregate further:

# Rank quality grades by their median-like percentile
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT *
    FROM (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    ) AS grade_stats
    WHERE n >= 50
    ORDER BY avg_sqft DESC;
""", conn)

title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
text:
12.5 Part 2 Exercises






NoteExercise 3: Scalar subquery — newest buildings





Write a query that finds all buildings built in the most recent year in the database (without hardcoding the year). How many are there?






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_newest,
           MAX(year_built) AS year
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built = (SELECT MAX(year_built) FROM buildings);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 4: WHERE NOT IN





Write a query that finds parcels (strap values) that have only non-residential building sections. Hint: find all parcels that appear in residential rows, then select parcels NOT IN that set.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT strap
    FROM buildings
    WHERE strap NOT IN (
        SELECT DISTINCT strap
        FROM buildings
        WHERE bldg_class = 'R'
    )
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
text:
12.6 Part 3: Common Table Expressions (CTEs)
A CTE is a named temporary result set defined at the top of a query with WITH ... AS. It makes complex queries readable by breaking them into named, sequential steps — the SQL equivalent of assigning intermediate DataFrames to variables in pandas.

12.6.1 The WITH … AS pattern
Compare these two approaches to the same question — “which quality grades have above-average square footage?”:
-- Subquery style (hard to read at depth)
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT quality_grade, AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings GROUP BY quality_grade
) WHERE avg_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings);

-- CTE style (name the intermediate result)
WITH grade_stats AS (
    SELECT quality_grade, AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings
    GROUP BY quality_grade
)
SELECT *
FROM grade_stats
WHERE avg_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings);
Both produce the same result. The CTE version is easier to read, debug, and extend.


12.6.2 Single CTE

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH grade_stats AS (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft,
            ROUND(AVG(year_built), 0) AS avg_year
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    )
    SELECT *
    FROM grade_stats
    WHERE n >= 50
    ORDER BY avg_sqft DESC;
""", conn)



12.6.3 Chained CTEs: multi-step analytical pipeline
You can define multiple CTEs separated by commas, each referencing the ones defined before it:

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH era_stats AS (
        SELECT
            (year_built / 10) * 10 AS decade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY decade
    ),
    overall AS (
        SELECT ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS grand_avg
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    )
    SELECT
        e.decade,
        e.n,
        e.avg_sqft,
        o.grand_avg,
        ROUND(e.avg_sqft - o.grand_avg, 0) AS diff_from_avg
    FROM era_stats e, overall o
    ORDER BY e.decade;
""", conn)

This pattern — define intermediate results as named CTEs, then combine them in a final SELECT — is the standard way to build complex SQL analyses. It makes every step visible and auditable.

title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
text:
12.7 Part 3 Exercises






NoteExercise 5: CTE — quality grade leaderboard





Write a CTE-based query that ranks quality grades by total square footage. Include the count of buildings, total square footage, and average square footage per building.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH grade_totals AS (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
            SUM(above_ground_sqft) AS total_sqft,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL AND above_ground_sqft IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    )
    SELECT *
    FROM grade_totals
    ORDER BY total_sqft DESC;
""", conn)













NoteExercise 6: Chained CTEs — era comparison





Write a query with two CTEs: one for pre-2000 buildings and one for 2000-and-later buildings. Compare the average square footage, count, and most common quality grade between the two eras.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH pre_2000 AS (
        SELECT
            'Pre-2000' AS era,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built < 2000 AND year_built IS NOT NULL
    ),
    post_2000 AS (
        SELECT
            '2000+' AS era,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built >= 2000 AND year_built IS NOT NULL
    )
    SELECT * FROM pre_2000
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM post_2000;
""", conn)
