title: 12  Extending Data with SQL
text:
12.9 Quick Reference

12.9.1 Subquery patterns

Scalar: returns one value → use in WHERE, SELECT, or HAVING
Column: returns one column → use with IN or NOT IN
Derived table: returns a table → use in FROM clause



12.9.2 CTE pattern
WITH step1 AS (
    SELECT ... FROM ... GROUP BY ...
),
step2 AS (
    SELECT ... FROM step1 WHERE ...
)
SELECT ... FROM step2;
CTEs are evaluated once, named for clarity, and discarded after the query completes. Use them whenever a query needs more than one level of aggregation or filtering.

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.1 Setup
This chapter completes the SQL module by joining the three cleaned Colorado public health tables into a single analysis-ready result set — one row per (county, year) — that can support rate calculations and cross-domain comparisons. The chapter is not just about the JOIN syntax. It is about the audit discipline that distinguishes a reliable join from a dangerous one: checking cardinality, identifying unmatched records, verifying row counts, and documenting every decision.
Joining tables is where the most consequential mistakes in data analysis happen — silently. A join that inflates row counts because of unexpected duplicates. A join that drops records because key formats do not match. A left join that introduces hundreds of NULL values you did not anticipate. The only defense is a systematic audit performed at every stage.
import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.2f}'.format)

conn = sqlite3.connect('colorado_public_health.db')
cursor = conn.cursor()
We are working with the three tables loaded and cleaned in Chapter 11: deaths (CDC mortality), crime (CBI offenses), and population (State Demography Office estimates). The county names have been standardized to a common format.

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.2 Part 1: Join Type Review
SQL supports four join types. Each determines what happens to rows that exist in one table but not the other.








Join type
Keeps
Use when




INNER JOIN
Only rows that match in both tables
You want complete cases only


LEFT JOIN
All rows from the left table; NULLs where the right has no match
You want to preserve the left table and add information where available


RIGHT JOIN
All rows from the right table; NULLs where the left has no match
Less common; same as LEFT JOIN with tables swapped


FULL OUTER JOIN
All rows from both tables; NULLs on both sides where no match
You want to see everything, including mismatches (not supported in SQLite)



For most analytical work, you will use LEFT JOIN (to preserve a base table and enrich it) or INNER JOIN (when you only want records present in all sources). The choice between them is a decision you must document — it determines which records appear in your analysis.

13.2.1 Toy example
To build intuition, consider two tiny tables:

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH left_t AS (
        SELECT 'Adams' AS county, 100 AS deaths UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Boulder', 200 UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Denver', 300
    ),
    right_t AS (
        SELECT 'Adams' AS county, 5000 AS pop UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Boulder', 3000 UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Costilla', 400
    )
    SELECT l.county AS l_county, l.deaths, r.county AS r_county, r.pop
    FROM left_t l
    LEFT JOIN right_t r ON l.county = r.county;
""", conn)

Denver appears with NULL population (no match in the right table). Costilla does not appear at all (it is only in the right table, and this is a LEFT JOIN). An INNER JOIN would drop Denver entirely.

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.3 Part 2: Cardinality Auditing
Before joining, you must understand the grain of each table — what combination of columns uniquely identifies a row — and verify that each table has exactly one row per key value. Joining tables with unexpected duplicates causes fan-out: row counts explode silently.

13.3.1 Grain of each table

# What combination of columns is the key for each table?
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT 'deaths' AS tbl,
           COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year) AS distinct_keys
    FROM deaths
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 'crime',
           COUNT(*),
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year)
    FROM crime
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 'population',
           COUNT(*),
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year)
    FROM population;
""", conn)

If total_rows exceeds distinct_keys, the table has multiple rows per (county, year) — which means it is at a finer grain than county-year. You must aggregate it down to county-year before joining, or the join will create duplicates.


13.3.2 Demonstrating fan-out

# If deaths has multiple rows per county-year (e.g., one per age group),
# joining directly to population creates duplicates
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT d.county, d.year, COUNT(*) AS n_rows
    FROM deaths d
    JOIN population p ON d.county = p.county AND d.year = p.year
    GROUP BY d.county, d.year
    HAVING n_rows > 1
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.4 Part 3: Unmatched Record Audit
Before joining, check which key values exist in one table but not the other. This tells you how many rows will get NULL values (in a LEFT JOIN) or be dropped entirely (in an INNER JOIN).

13.4.1 Anti-join: counties in one table but not another
An anti-join finds records in one table that have no match in the other. SQL does not have a dedicated anti-join keyword, but you can achieve it with LEFT JOIN ... WHERE right.key IS NULL or with NOT IN:

# Counties in deaths that are NOT in population
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT d.county
    FROM deaths d
    LEFT JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM population) p
        ON d.county = p.county
    WHERE p.county IS NULL
    ORDER BY d.county;
""", conn)

If this returns rows, those counties will have NULL population values after joining — which means you cannot compute rates for them. This is essential information to document.


13.4.2 Full cross-table mismatch audit

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH death_counties AS (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM deaths),
         crime_counties AS (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM crime),
         pop_counties AS (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM population)
    SELECT
        COALESCE(d.county, c.county, p.county) AS county,
        CASE WHEN d.county IS NOT NULL THEN 'Y' ELSE 'N' END AS in_deaths,
        CASE WHEN c.county IS NOT NULL THEN 'Y' ELSE 'N' END AS in_crime,
        CASE WHEN p.county IS NOT NULL THEN 'Y' ELSE 'N' END AS in_pop
    FROM death_counties d
    FULL OUTER JOIN crime_counties c ON d.county = c.county
    FULL OUTER JOIN pop_counties p ON COALESCE(d.county, c.county) = p.county
    ORDER BY county;
""", conn)







Note



SQLite does not support FULL OUTER JOIN. To achieve this in SQLite, use a UNION of left joins or create a reference table of all distinct counties and left-join each source table to it.

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.5 Part 4: Granularity Alignment
Each table may have a finer grain than county-year. Before joining, aggregate each table to the target grain — one row per (county, year).

13.5.1 Aggregate deaths to county-year

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths, COUNT(*) AS n_detail_rows
    FROM deaths
    GROUP BY county, year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)



13.5.2 Aggregate crime to county-year

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses
    FROM crime
    GROUP BY county, year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)



13.5.3 Aggregate population to county-year

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop
    FROM population
    GROUP BY county, year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)



13.5.4 Verify uniqueness after aggregation

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg_deaths AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths
        FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year
    )
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year) AS distinct_keys
    FROM agg_deaths;
""", conn)

Total rows and distinct keys should be equal. If they are not, the aggregation did not produce a unique key and the join will still fan out.

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.6 Part 5: Building the Analysis-Ready Dataset
With each table aggregated to county-year and cardinality verified, we can now join them.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg_deaths AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths
        FROM deaths
        GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_crime AS (
        SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses
        FROM crime
        GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_pop AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop
        FROM population
        GROUP BY county, year
    )
    SELECT
        COALESCE(d.county, c.county, p.county) AS county,
        COALESCE(d.year, c.year, p.year) AS year,
        d.total_deaths,
        c.total_offenses,
        p.total_pop,
        ROUND(1.0 * d.total_deaths / p.total_pop * 100000, 1) AS death_rate_per_100k,
        ROUND(1.0 * c.total_offenses / p.total_pop * 100000, 1) AS crime_rate_per_100k
    FROM agg_pop p
    LEFT JOIN agg_deaths d ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
    LEFT JOIN agg_crime c ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    ORDER BY county, year
    LIMIT 20;
""", conn)

The population table serves as the base (left) table because it has the most complete county-year coverage. Deaths and crime data are joined onto it. Counties or years with no death or crime records will show NULL in those columns — a known and documented gap, not an error.

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.7 Part 6: Post-Join Audit
After building the joined dataset, run a systematic audit.

13.7.1 Row count audit

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg_pop AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop
        FROM population GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_deaths AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths
        FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_crime AS (
        SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses
        FROM crime GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    joined AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year, d.total_deaths, c.total_offenses, p.total_pop
        FROM agg_pop p
        LEFT JOIN agg_deaths d ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        LEFT JOIN agg_crime c ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    )
    SELECT
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM agg_pop) AS pop_rows,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM agg_deaths) AS death_rows,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM agg_crime) AS crime_rows,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM joined) AS joined_rows;
""", conn)

The joined row count should equal the population row count (the base table in a LEFT JOIN). If it is larger, you have a fan-out problem. If it is smaller, something went wrong with the join.


13.7.2 Totals audit

# Verify that aggregated totals in the joined result match the source tables
pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH joined AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year,
               d.total_deaths, c.total_offenses, p.total_pop
        FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
            ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses FROM crime GROUP BY county, year) c
            ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    )
    SELECT
        SUM(total_pop) AS joined_pop_total,
        SUM(total_deaths) AS joined_death_total,
        SUM(total_offenses) AS joined_crime_total;
""", conn)

Compare these totals to SELECT SUM(...) FROM each source table individually. Any discrepancy indicates a join problem.


13.7.3 NULL audit

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH joined AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year,
               d.total_deaths, c.total_offenses, p.total_pop
        FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
            ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses FROM crime GROUP BY county, year) c
            ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    )
    SELECT
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
        SUM(CASE WHEN total_deaths IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_deaths,
        SUM(CASE WHEN total_offenses IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_crime,
        SUM(CASE WHEN total_pop IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_pop
    FROM joined;
""", conn)

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.8 Part 7: The Join-Quality Report
Every multi-table analysis should produce a brief report documenting the join. This is the SQL equivalent of the Known Issues Log from Chapter 3. At minimum, it should cover:

Join structure: which tables, which keys, which join type, and why
Granularity alignment: how each table was aggregated to the target grain
Cardinality checks: confirmation that each table has one row per key after aggregation
Unmatched record audit: which counties/years are missing from each source
Row count audit: joined rows vs. base table rows
Totals audit: aggregated values in joined result vs. source tables
NULL summary: how many NULLs were introduced by the join
Remaining uncertainties: what you could not verify or what might affect interpretation

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.9 Pandas Cross-Check
A valuable validation practice is to replicate the SQL join in pandas and compare results:

# Read the joined result from SQL
sql_result = pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT p.county, p.year,
           SUM(d.total_deaths) AS total_deaths,
           SUM(p.total_pop) AS total_pop
    FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
    LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
        ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
    GROUP BY p.county, p.year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

sql_result


# Replicate in pandas
# deaths_df = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM deaths", conn)
# pop_df = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM population", conn)
# ... aggregate and merge in pandas, then compare

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.10 Post-Join Audit Checklist
Use this checklist after every multi-table join:

Row count: joined rows == base table rows (for LEFT JOIN)
No fan-out: no (county, year) appears more than once
Totals match: SUM(col) in joined result matches source tables
NULLs explained: every NULL column has a documented reason
Cross-check: pandas merge produces the same result
Report written: join-quality report documents decisions and uncertainties

title: 13  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
13.11 Join Type Decision Guide








Situation
Join type
Rationale




You need all records from your base table
LEFT JOIN
Preserves base; adds NULLs where no match


You need only complete cases
INNER JOIN
Drops any row missing from either table


You want to see what is missing from each side
Anti-join pattern
LEFT JOIN + WHERE IS NULL


You need all records from both sides
FULL OUTER JOIN
Not available in SQLite; simulate with UNION

text:
13.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: INNER JOIN vs. LEFT JOIN impact





Write both an INNER JOIN and a LEFT JOIN version of the three-table join. How many rows does each produce? What counties or years are lost in the INNER JOIN?






NoteSolution





Compare SELECT COUNT(*) from each version. The difference represents county-year combinations present in population but absent from deaths or crime. Document which counties are affected and why.












NoteExercise 2: Top 10 counties by death rate





Using the analysis-ready dataset, find the top 10 counties by death rate per 100,000 in the most recent year available. Only include counties with population greater than 1,000 to avoid extreme rates from tiny counties.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year, SUM(d.deaths) AS total_deaths, SUM(p.totalpopulation) AS total_pop
        FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS totalpopulation FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
            ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        GROUP BY p.county, p.year
    )
    SELECT county, year, total_deaths, total_pop,
           ROUND(1.0 * total_deaths / total_pop * 100000, 1) AS death_rate
    FROM agg
    WHERE year = (SELECT MAX(year) FROM agg)
      AND total_pop > 1000
      AND total_deaths IS NOT NULL
    ORDER BY death_rate DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

Write a complete join-quality report for the three-table join you built in this chapter. Follow the seven-section structure described in Part 7. Be specific about numbers — how many rows, how many NULLs, which counties are affected.

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
14.1 What Makes a Good Research Question?
The previous parts of this book taught you to load, clean, extend, and reshape data. Those are essential mechanical skills. But mechanics alone do not produce insight — you also need to ask the right questions and understand what your data can and cannot tell you. This chapter introduces the conceptual vocabulary for formulating research questions with tabular data, recognizing the structure of the comparisons your data supports, and identifying the limitations that prevent you from drawing conclusions the data does not warrant.
None of this requires new code. It requires new thinking.
A good research question is specific enough to be answered with data and interesting enough to be worth answering. Compare these two questions:
A useful template is: “Is there a difference in [outcome] between [groups] / over [time] / across [conditions]?” The components of this template — outcome, groups, time, conditions — map directly to columns in your data. If you cannot point to specific columns that represent each component, the question is not yet answerable with the data you have.
crumbs: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
“Is baseball different now?” — too vague to answer. Different how? Compared to when?
“Has the league-wide home run rate per at-bat changed between the 1990s and the 2010s?” — specific, measurable, and answerable with the Lahman batting data.
crumbs: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
14.2 The Unit of Analysis
Before you can answer any question, you need to know what a single row in your dataset represents. This is the unit of analysis, and getting it wrong is one of the most common sources of error in data work.
In the Lahman batting data, each row is a player-season-stint: one player’s statistics for one team in one season. If you compute the average batting average across all rows, you are computing the average across player-seasons — which weights a player with 10 at-bats the same as a player with 600. That is almost certainly not what you intend.
In the Colorado population data, each row is a county-year-age: one county’s population for one single year of age in one year. If you sum totalpopulation without grouping by county and year first, you will add up every age group and get a number far larger than any county’s actual population.
The discipline from Parts II and III — always checking .shape, always understanding what a row represents — is not just a cleaning habit. It is the foundation of valid analysis.








Dataset
Unit of analysis
Common mistake




Lahman Batting
Player-team-season
Averaging rates without weighting by at-bats


DOLA Population
County-year-age
Summing population without grouping by county-year


Cannabis Sales
County-month-year
Treating monthly data as annual without aggregation


CDC Deaths
County-year-age group-cause
Double-counting deaths across cause categories
crumbs: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
14.3 Between-Subjects vs. Within-Subjects Comparisons
Most analytical questions involve comparisons, and the structure of the comparison determines which methods are appropriate.
Between-subjects (or between-groups) comparisons look at different entities measured under different conditions. “Do urban counties have higher crime rates than rural counties?” compares different counties — each county is measured once, and the comparison is across groups. In tabular data, this means filtering to different subsets of rows and comparing an outcome column.
Within-subjects (or repeated-measures) comparisons look at the same entity measured at multiple time points or under multiple conditions. “Has Boulder County’s dependency ratio changed from 2000 to 2020?” tracks a single county across time. In tabular data, this means filtering to one entity and examining how an outcome column changes across another column (usually time).
The distinction matters because between-subjects comparisons are confounded by all the ways the groups differ beyond the variable of interest. Urban and rural counties differ in population density, but they also differ in demographics, economic base, healthcare access, and dozens of other factors. A within-subjects comparison controls for many of these differences (Boulder County’s geography does not change between 2000 and 2020), but introduces the possibility that other things changed simultaneously (state policy, national trends).

14.3.1 Recognizing the structure in your data
Ask yourself: “Am I comparing different entities, or the same entity at different times?”

import pandas as pd

# Between-subjects: compare counties in a single year
# Each county appears once → between-subjects
# pop_2020 = pop[pop['year'] == 2020].groupby('county')['totalpopulation'].sum()

# Within-subjects: track one county across years
# Same county appears multiple times → within-subjects
# boulder_trend = pop[pop['county'] == 'Boulder'].groupby('year')['totalpopulation'].sum()
crumbs: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
14.4 Cross-Sectional vs. Longitudinal Analysis
A closely related distinction is whether your analysis examines one point in time or multiple points.
Cross-sectional analysis looks at a snapshot: all counties in 2020, all players in the 2022 season. It is good for describing the current state of things and identifying patterns across entities, but it cannot tell you about change over time.
Longitudinal analysis tracks the same entities across time: Boulder County from 1990 to 2024, league-wide batting averages from 1900 to 2022. It can reveal trends, turning points, and the effects of interventions (a new policy, a rule change), but it requires careful handling of time-related confounds.
Most of the datasets in this book support both types of analysis. The cannabis sales data can be analyzed cross-sectionally (which counties had the highest sales in 2023?) or longitudinally (how have Denver’s sales changed since legalization?). The choice depends on the question, not the data.

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
section: 14.5 Observational Data and the Limits of Causation
text:
14.5 Observational Data and the Limits of Causation
Every dataset in this book is observational — the data was collected by recording what happened, not by experimentally manipulating conditions and measuring the effect. This is the norm in data analysis outside of controlled experiments, and it imposes a fundamental limitation: observational data can show associations, but it cannot prove causation.
Consider the merged Lahman dataset from Chapter 9, where you found that higher-paid players tend to have better batting statistics. Can you conclude that paying players more causes them to hit better? No. Teams pay more for players who have already demonstrated high performance. The causal arrow likely runs from performance to salary, not the other way around. And both might be influenced by a third factor — age, position, market size — that you have not accounted for.
This does not mean observational analysis is useless. It means you must be disciplined about the language you use. Say “counties with higher population density tend to have higher crime rates” rather than “high population density causes higher crime rates.” The first is a description of a pattern in the data. The second is a causal claim that the data cannot support.

14.5.1 Common confounds in the course datasets







Apparent relationship
Possible confound




Higher salary → better batting stats
Teams pay for past performance; age and experience drive both


More cannabis sales → more crime
Both correlate with county population; urban counties have more of both


Higher dependency ratio → lower income
Rural counties have both older populations and less economic diversity


More deaths → larger county
Death counts scale with population; rates are the meaningful comparison



The last example deserves emphasis. Comparing raw counts across groups of different sizes is one of the most common analytical mistakes. Denver will always have more deaths, more crimes, and more cannabis sales than San Juan County — because Denver has 700,000 people and San Juan has 700. The meaningful comparison is the rate: deaths per 100,000 population, crimes per capita, sales per resident. Computing rates requires the population data, which is why the multi-table join in Chapter 13 is not just a technical exercise — it is an analytical necessity.

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
14.6 Formulating Your Question
Before writing any analytical code, answer these four questions in a Markdown cell:

What is the outcome I am measuring? (Which column? What does a higher or lower value mean?)
What am I comparing? (Groups? Time periods? Conditions?)
What is the unit of analysis? (What does each row represent? Do I need to aggregate first?)
What can I conclude vs. what can I only speculate about? (Association or causation? What confounds exist?)

This takes two minutes and prevents hours of misguided analysis. The remaining chapters in Part IV assume you have done this work before running any code.

title: 14  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
14.7 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Classify the comparison





For each question below, identify whether it is a between-subjects or within-subjects comparison, and whether it is cross-sectional or longitudinal.

Do National League teams hit more home runs than American League teams in 2022?
Has Colorado’s statewide dependency ratio increased from 2000 to 2020?
Is there a correlation between county population and cannabis sales in 2023?
Did batting averages decline after the introduction of the designated hitter rule?







NoteSolution






Between-subjects (different teams), cross-sectional (one season). b. Within-subjects (same state), longitudinal (multiple years). c. Between-subjects (different counties), cross-sectional (one year). d. Within-subjects in a sense (same league), longitudinal (before/after a rule change) — though it is comparing different players across eras, making it partly between-subjects too.













NoteExercise 2: Identify the confound





A colleague shows you a scatter plot demonstrating that Colorado counties with more cannabis dispensaries also have more traffic accidents. They conclude that cannabis causes traffic accidents. Write a paragraph identifying at least two confounds and explaining why this conclusion is not supported by the data.






NoteSolution





Both dispensary count and traffic accidents correlate with county population — larger counties have more of everything. They also correlate with urbanization, road density, and commuter traffic. Without controlling for population (converting to per-capita rates) and other covariates, the correlation between dispensaries and accidents may be entirely explained by the fact that both are proxies for county size. Observational data like this can show association but cannot establish causation.

Choose one of the course datasets and write a research question using the template from this chapter. Then answer the four framing questions (outcome, comparison, unit of analysis, limitations). Be specific enough that another student could run the analysis from your description alone.

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.1 Setup
Before you compare groups or test hypotheses, you need to understand what your data looks like. Descriptive exploration is the process of characterizing a dataset’s central tendencies, variability, shape, and outliers — building an informed mental model of the data that guides every subsequent analytical decision. This chapter goes beyond the .describe() method you have already used and introduces systematic techniques for understanding distributions, identifying unusual observations, examining categorical patterns, and constructing frequency tables and cross-tabulations.
The philosophy here is qualitative in the broadest sense: you are looking for patterns, surprises, and anomalies rather than computing formal test statistics. The goal is to know your data well enough that nothing in the later chapters catches you off guard.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)

sns.set_style('whitegrid')

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.2 Datasets
We continue with the datasets from earlier chapters, loading and preparing them with the derived columns already established.

# Lahman batting with derived metrics
batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']).replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

# Filter to qualified player-seasons for meaningful rate statistics
qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100].copy()

print('Qualified player-seasons:', qualified.shape[0])


# Colorado population estimates
url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/co-publicdata/sya-county.csv'
pop = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
estimates = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()


# Cannabis sales (cleaned)
cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2, skipfooter=8, engine='python', na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical', 'retail']
cannabis['county'] = cannabis['county'].str.strip()
for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False),
        errors='coerce'
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['county'] != 'Total']

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.3 Beyond .describe(): Understanding Distributions
The .describe() method gives you the five-number summary (min, 25th percentile, median, 75th percentile, max) plus the mean and count. This is a useful starting point, but it hides important features of a distribution: its shape, its skewness, and whether it has multiple modes.

15.3.1 Skewness
Many real-world variables are right-skewed — a long tail extends toward high values while most observations cluster near the low end. Income, city populations, and home prices all follow this pattern. Cannabis sales across Colorado counties are a clear example:

annual_sales = (
    cannabis[cannabis['year'] == 2023]
    .groupby('county')['retail']
    .sum()
    .dropna()
)

print(annual_sales.describe())
print(f'\nSkewness: {annual_sales.skew():.2f}')
print(f'Mean:     ${annual_sales.mean():,.0f}')
print(f'Median:   ${annual_sales.median():,.0f}')

When the mean is substantially larger than the median, the distribution is right-skewed, and the mean is being pulled upward by a few very large values. In this case, the median is a better measure of the “typical” county than the mean.


15.3.2 Quantiles and percentiles
Quantiles divide your data into equal-sized groups. The .quantile() method lets you compute arbitrary percentiles:

# Decile boundaries for batting average
percentiles = qualified['BA'].quantile(np.arange(0, 1.1, 0.1))
print('Batting average deciles:')
print(percentiles.round(3))

Quantiles are useful for creating bins (as with pd.qcut() in Chapter 9), for identifying where a specific value falls in the distribution, and for detecting whether the distribution has unusual gaps or concentrations.


15.3.3 The coefficient of variation
Standard deviation tells you how spread out values are, but its meaning depends on the scale of the variable. A standard deviation of 1,000 is enormous for a variable with a mean of 500 but trivial for a variable with a mean of 10,000,000. The coefficient of variation (CV) — the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean — provides a scale-free measure of relative variability:

for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    mean_val = cannabis[col].mean()
    std_val = cannabis[col].std()
    cv = std_val / mean_val
    print(f'{col}: mean={mean_val:,.0f}, std={std_val:,.0f}, CV={cv:.2f}')

A CV above 1.0 indicates very high variability relative to the mean — typical of skewed distributions.

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.4 Identifying Outliers
An outlier is an observation that is unusually far from the rest of the data. Outliers are not automatically errors — they may be legitimate extreme values that are analytically important. The goal is to identify them, investigate them, and make a documented decision about whether to include them in your analysis.

15.4.1 The IQR method
The interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the 25th and 75th percentiles. The standard convention defines outliers as values below Q1 − 1.5 × IQR or above Q3 + 1.5 × IQR:

def find_outliers_iqr(series, multiplier=1.5):
    """Identify outliers using the IQR method."""
    Q1 = series.quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = series.quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower = Q1 - multiplier * IQR
    upper = Q3 + multiplier * IQR

    outliers = series[(series < lower) | (series > upper)]

    print(f'Q1={Q1:.3f}, Q3={Q3:.3f}, IQR={IQR:.3f}')
    print(f'Lower fence: {lower:.3f}, Upper fence: {upper:.3f}')
    print(f'Outliers: {len(outliers)} of {len(series)} ({len(outliers)/len(series):.1%})')

    return outliers

hr_outliers = find_outliers_iqr(qualified['HR'])



15.4.2 The z-score method
A z-score measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean. Values with |z| > 3 are conventionally considered outliers:

qualified['hr_zscore'] = (
    (qualified['HR'] - qualified['HR'].mean()) / qualified['HR'].std()
)

extreme = qualified[qualified['hr_zscore'].abs() > 3]
print(f'Observations with |z| > 3: {len(extreme)}')
print(extreme[['playerID', 'yearID', 'HR', 'hr_zscore']].sort_values('hr_zscore', ascending=False).head(10))



15.4.3 Investigating outliers
Once you identify outliers, investigate them before deciding what to do. Are they data entry errors, measurement artifacts, or genuinely extreme observations?

# Are the extreme HR seasons real or errors?
# Check: do the player names and years match known record holders?
print(extreme[['playerID', 'yearID', 'teamID', 'HR']].sort_values('HR', ascending=False).head(5))

If these are legitimate records (Barry Bonds’ 73 HR in 2001, for instance), removing them would be analytically dishonest. If they are data entry errors (a player with 999 HR), removing them is necessary. Document the decision either way.

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.5 Categorical Patterns
For non-numeric columns, the primary descriptive tools are frequency tables and cross-tabulations.

15.5.1 Frequency tables
The .value_counts() method is your workhorse for categorical exploration. Use it with normalize=True to get proportions instead of counts:

# County distribution of cannabis sales records
county_counts = cannabis['county'].value_counts()
print(f'Counties with data: {len(county_counts)}')
print(f'\nTop 10:')
print(county_counts.head(10))
print(f'\nBottom 5:')
print(county_counts.tail(5))


# Age band distribution in Colorado (2020)
bins = [0, 18, 65, 101]
labels = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']
est_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020].copy()
est_2020['age_band'] = pd.cut(est_2020['age'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)

band_totals = est_2020.groupby('age_band', observed=True)['totalpopulation'].sum()
print('Statewide population by age band (2020):')
print(band_totals)
print(f'\nProportions:')
print((band_totals / band_totals.sum()).round(3))



15.5.2 Cross-tabulations
A cross-tabulation (crosstab) shows the joint distribution of two categorical variables. Use pd.crosstab() to build one:

# Decades × league for batting data
batting_with_league = batting.copy()

decade_counts = pd.crosstab(
    qualified['decade'],
    qualified['teamID'].str[:2],  # rough proxy
    margins=True
)
print('Player-seasons by decade (first 5 decades):')
print(decade_counts.head())

Cross-tabulations become more informative with normalize to show proportions and with margins=True to include row and column totals.

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.6 Summarizing Multiple Variables Together
When you have several numeric columns, examining them individually is tedious. A correlation matrix provides a compact summary of pairwise relationships:

batting_metrics = qualified[['AB', 'H', 'HR', 'BB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS']].copy()

corr_matrix = batting_metrics.corr().round(2)
print(corr_matrix)

We will visualize this matrix as a heatmap in Chapter 16 and test individual correlations formally in Chapter 17.

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.7 The Descriptive Summary Template
For any new dataset or analytical question, produce a descriptive summary that covers these elements. You can structure this as a Markdown cell in your notebook:
## Descriptive Summary: [Dataset / Variable]

**Unit of analysis:** [what each row represents]
**N:** [number of observations after filtering]
**Key statistics:** mean, median, std, min, max
**Shape:** [symmetric, right-skewed, bimodal, etc.]
**Outliers:** [count, method used, decision made]
**Missing data:** [count, pattern, impact on analysis]
**Notable patterns:** [any surprises, clusters, or anomalies]

title: 15  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
15.8 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Distribution profile





Compute the mean, median, standard deviation, skewness, and coefficient of variation for retail cannabis sales in 2023 across all counties. Based on these statistics, describe the shape of the distribution in one or two sentences. Which measure of central tendency (mean or median) better represents the “typical” county?






NoteSolution






annual = cannabis[cannabis['year'] == 2023].groupby('county')['retail'].sum().dropna()
print(f'Mean:     ${annual.mean():,.0f}')
print(f'Median:   ${annual.median():,.0f}')
print(f'Std:      ${annual.std():,.0f}')
print(f'Skewness: {annual.skew():.2f}')
print(f'CV:       {annual.std() / annual.mean():.2f}')

The distribution is strongly right-skewed (skewness >> 1), with a few large urban counties pulling the mean well above the median. The median better represents the typical county.












NoteExercise 2: Outlier investigation





Using the IQR method, identify outlier counties in 2023 retail cannabis sales. For each outlier, look up the county’s population. Are these outliers because they are genuinely unusual, or simply because they are large counties?






NoteSolution






outliers = find_outliers_iqr(annual)
print('Outlier counties:')
print(outliers.sort_values(ascending=False))

These are almost certainly large urban counties (Denver, El Paso, Arapahoe, etc.). They are outliers in sales volume because they have more people, not because their per-capita sales are unusual. Computing per-capita sales (by dividing by population) would likely eliminate most of these as outliers.












NoteExercise 3: Cross-tabulation





Create a cross-tabulation of cannabis counties by year and whether they have any medical sales (yes/no). Has the number of counties reporting medical sales changed over time?






NoteSolution






cannabis['has_medical'] = cannabis['medical'].notna() & (cannabis['medical'] > 0)
crosstab = pd.crosstab(cannabis['year'], cannabis['has_medical'], margins=True)
crosstab.columns = ['No Medical', 'Has Medical', 'Total']
print(crosstab)

title: 16  Visual Exploration
text:
16.1 Setup
A well-chosen visualization reveals structure that no table of numbers can convey: the shape of a distribution, the trajectory of a trend, the clustering of groups, the isolation of outliers. This chapter introduces a systematic repertoire of plot types for exploratory data analysis using matplotlib and seaborn, organized by what you are trying to see — distributions, relationships, trends, and comparisons. The goal is not to produce publication-ready graphics (though some of these will be close) but to build visual intuitions that inform your analytical decisions.
Every plot in this chapter answers a question. Before writing any plotting code, state the question in a Markdown cell. After producing the plot, write one or two sentences interpreting what it shows. This habit turns exploratory plots from pretty pictures into analytical evidence.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)

# Seaborn defaults: clean, readable, and appropriately sized
sns.set_style('whitegrid')
sns.set_context('notebook', font_scale=1.1)

title: 16  Visual Exploration
text:
16.2 Datasets

# Lahman batting — qualified player-seasons
batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']).replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10
qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100].copy()

# Salaries
salaries = pd.read_csv('Salaries.csv')
bat_sal = pd.merge(
    qualified, salaries[['playerID', 'yearID', 'salary']],
    on=['playerID', 'yearID'], how='inner'
)


# Colorado population
url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/co-publicdata/sya-county.csv'
pop = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
estimates = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
estimates['age_band'] = pd.cut(
    estimates['age'], bins=[0, 18, 65, 101],
    labels=['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)'],
    right=False
)


# Cannabis sales
cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2, skipfooter=8, engine='python', na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical', 'retail']
cannabis['county'] = cannabis['county'].str.strip()
for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False),
        errors='coerce'
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['county'] != 'Total']

title: 16  Visual Exploration
text:
16.3 Visualizing Distributions
The first question to ask about any numeric variable is: what does its distribution look like? Is it symmetric, skewed, bimodal, or uniform? Distributions guide your choice of summary statistics (mean vs. median), your approach to outliers, and your selection of statistical tests.

16.3.1 Histograms
A histogram divides the range of values into bins and counts how many observations fall in each. It is the most direct visualization of a distribution’s shape.

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.hist(qualified['BA'], bins=50, edgecolor='white', alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
ax.set_ylabel('Count')
ax.set_title('Distribution of Batting Average (qualified player-seasons, AB ≥ 100)')
ax.axvline(qualified['BA'].mean(), color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean: {qualified["BA"].mean():.3f}')
ax.axvline(qualified['BA'].median(), color='orange', linestyle='--', label=f'Median: {qualified["BA"].median():.3f}')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The mean and median are close together, suggesting a roughly symmetric distribution. If they diverged substantially, the distribution would be skewed.


16.3.2 Kernel density estimation (KDE)
A KDE plot smooths the histogram into a continuous curve, making it easier to see the overall shape:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.kdeplot(data=qualified, x='BA', ax=ax, fill=True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_title('KDE of Batting Average')
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



16.3.3 Comparing distributions across groups
Overlaying distributions for different groups reveals how they differ. Here we compare batting averages across eras:

era_filter = qualified['decade'].isin([1920, 1960, 2000])
era_data = qualified[era_filter].copy()
era_data['era'] = era_data['decade'].map({1920: '1920s', 1960: '1960s', 2000: '2000s'})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.kdeplot(data=era_data, x='BA', hue='era', ax=ax, fill=True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_title('Batting Average Distribution by Era')
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



16.3.4 Box plots
Box plots show the five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max) plus individual outlier points. They are especially effective for comparing distributions across many groups:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
decade_data = qualified[qualified['decade'] >= 1900]
sns.boxplot(data=decade_data, x='decade', y='BA', ax=ax, color='lightblue')
ax.set_title('Batting Average by Decade (AB ≥ 100)')
ax.set_xlabel('Decade')
ax.set_ylabel('Batting Average')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The boxes show the interquartile range (middle 50%), the horizontal line is the median, and the dots are outliers beyond the IQR fences.


16.3.5 Violin plots
A violin plot combines a box plot with a KDE, showing the full shape of each group’s distribution:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
selected_decades = decade_data[decade_data['decade'].isin([1920, 1950, 1980, 2010])]
sns.violinplot(data=selected_decades, x='decade', y='HR', ax=ax, inner='quartile')
ax.set_title('Home Run Distribution by Selected Decades')
ax.set_xlabel('Decade')
ax.set_ylabel('Home Runs')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: 16  Visual Exploration
text:
16.4 Visualizing Relationships
When you want to understand how two numeric variables relate to each other, scatter plots and correlation heatmaps are your primary tools.

16.4.1 Scatter plots

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
sample = bat_sal[bat_sal['yearID'] == 2015].copy()
ax.scatter(sample['salary'] / 1e6, sample['OPS'], alpha=0.4, s=20)
ax.set_xlabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_ylabel('OPS')
ax.set_title('Salary vs. OPS (2015 season, qualified batters)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

If the points form a rising cloud, the variables are positively correlated. If the cloud is flat or shapeless, there is little linear relationship. If the cloud curves, the relationship may be nonlinear — something a correlation coefficient alone would not reveal.


16.4.2 Adding context with color and size
Seaborn’s scatterplot() can encode additional variables using color (hue) and size:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
sns.scatterplot(
    data=sample, x=sample['salary']/1e6, y='OPS',
    hue='AB', size='HR', sizes=(20, 200), alpha=0.6, ax=ax
)
ax.set_xlabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_title('Salary vs. OPS — sized by HR, colored by AB')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



16.4.3 Pair plots
When you have several numeric variables, a pair plot shows every pairwise scatter plot and each variable’s distribution in a single figure:

pair_cols = ['BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'HR']
pair_data = qualified[qualified['decade'] == 2010][pair_cols].dropna().sample(500, random_state=42)

sns.pairplot(pair_data, diag_kind='kde', plot_kws={'alpha': 0.3, 's': 15})
plt.suptitle('Pairwise Relationships — 2010s Batting (sample)', y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



16.4.4 Correlation heatmaps
A heatmap visualizes the correlation matrix from Chapter 15, making it easy to spot strong relationships at a glance:

metrics = qualified[['AB', 'H', 'HR', 'BB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS']].dropna()
corr = metrics.corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', center=0, ax=ax,
            square=True, linewidths=0.5)
ax.set_title('Correlation Matrix — Batting Metrics')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Red cells indicate strong positive correlations, blue cells indicate negative correlations, and white cells indicate little linear relationship.

title: 16  Visual Exploration
text:
16.5 Visualizing Trends Over Time
Time series plots — line charts showing how a variable changes across time — are essential for longitudinal analysis.

16.5.1 Single trend line

# Statewide cannabis retail sales by year
annual_state = cannabis.groupby('year')['retail'].sum().reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(annual_state['year'], annual_state['retail'] / 1e9, marker='o')
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Retail Sales ($ billions)')
ax.set_title('Colorado Statewide Retail Cannabis Sales')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



16.5.2 Multiple trend lines for comparison

# Compare top 5 counties
top_counties = (
    cannabis.groupby('county')['retail'].sum()
    .nlargest(5).index.tolist()
)
top_data = cannabis[cannabis['county'].isin(top_counties)]
county_annual = top_data.groupby(['year', 'county'])['retail'].sum().reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.lineplot(data=county_annual, x='year', y='retail', hue='county', marker='o', ax=ax)
ax.set_ylabel('Annual Retail Sales ($)')
ax.set_title('Retail Cannabis Sales — Top 5 Counties')
ax.legend(title='County', bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



16.5.3 Trends with confidence bands
Seaborn can compute and display confidence intervals around aggregated trends:

decade_ba = qualified[qualified['decade'] >= 1900]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.lineplot(data=decade_ba, x='yearID', y='BA', ax=ax, ci=95)
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Batting Average')
ax.set_title('League-Wide Batting Average Over Time (95% CI)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The shaded band shows the 95% confidence interval around the mean at each year, giving you a sense of both the trend and the variability.

title: 16  Visual Exploration
text:
16.6 Visualizing Comparisons
When you want to compare a metric across categorical groups, bar charts, grouped bar charts, and faceted plots make the differences visible.

16.6.1 Bar charts for group comparisons

# Dependency ratio by county type (top vs bottom 10)
dep_wide = (
    estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020]
    .groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True)
    .agg(pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum'))
    .reset_index()
    .pivot_table(index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop', aggfunc='sum')
    .reset_index()
)
dep_wide.columns.name = None
dep_wide['dep_ratio'] = (
    (dep_wide['Children (0-17)'] + dep_wide['Seniors (65+)'])
    / dep_wide['Working Age (18-64)']
)

top5 = dep_wide.nlargest(5, 'dep_ratio')
bot5 = dep_wide.nsmallest(5, 'dep_ratio')
comparison = pd.concat([top5, bot5]).sort_values('dep_ratio', ascending=True)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
colors = ['steelblue' if x in bot5['county'].values else 'coral' for x in comparison['county']]
ax.barh(comparison['county'], comparison['dep_ratio'], color=colors)
ax.set_xlabel('Dependency Ratio')
ax.set_title('Highest and Lowest Dependency Ratios — Colorado Counties (2020)')
ax.axvline(dep_wide['dep_ratio'].median(), color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='Median')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



16.6.2 Faceted plots (small multiples)
Faceting breaks a single plot into a grid of subplots, one per group. This is one of the most effective techniques for comparing patterns across categories:

# Batting average distribution across four selected decades
four_decades = qualified[qualified['decade'].isin([1930, 1960, 1990, 2020])].copy()
four_decades['era'] = four_decades['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

g = sns.FacetGrid(four_decades, col='era', col_wrap=2, height=3.5, aspect=1.3)
g.map(sns.histplot, 'BA', bins=30, kde=True)
g.set_axis_labels('Batting Average', 'Count')
g.set_titles('{col_name}')
g.figure.suptitle('Batting Average Distribution by Era', y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

text:
16.7 Choosing the Right Plot








Question
Plot type
When to use




What does this variable look like?
Histogram, KDE, box plot
Single numeric variable


How do distributions differ across groups?
Overlaid KDE, grouped box plot, violin plot
One numeric + one categorical


How are two variables related?
Scatter plot, pair plot
Two numeric variables


What are all pairwise relationships?
Correlation heatmap, pair plot
Many numeric variables


How has a variable changed over time?
Line plot
Numeric variable + time


How do groups compare on a metric?
Bar chart, grouped bar chart
One numeric + one categorical


How do patterns vary across subgroups?
Faceted plot (small multiples)
Any plot + a grouping variable

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.1 Setup
Descriptive statistics and visualizations tell you what your data looks like. Hypothesis testing tells you whether the patterns you see are likely to be meaningful or whether they could have arisen by chance. This chapter introduces a small set of statistical tests — enough to make principled statements about group differences and variable relationships — and focuses on interpreting results in plain language rather than on statistical theory.
The framework is deliberately simple. You will learn to ask three questions: Is there a difference between these groups? How strong is the relationship between these variables? Could what I’m seeing be due to random variation? The tools that answer these questions — t-tests, chi-square tests, and correlation coefficients — cover a large proportion of the exploratory comparisons you will encounter with tabular data.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)
sns.set_style('whitegrid')

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.2 Datasets

# Lahman batting — qualified player-seasons with salary
batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']).replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10
qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100].copy()

salaries = pd.read_csv('Salaries.csv')
bat_sal = pd.merge(
    qualified, salaries[['playerID', 'yearID', 'salary']],
    on=['playerID', 'yearID'], how='inner'
)


# Cannabis sales
cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2, skipfooter=8, engine='python', na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical', 'retail']
cannabis['county'] = cannabis['county'].str.strip()
for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False),
        errors='coerce'
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['county'] != 'Total']

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.3 The Logic of Hypothesis Testing
Every hypothesis test follows the same structure:

State the null hypothesis (H₀): There is no difference between the groups, or there is no relationship between the variables. The observed pattern is due to chance.
State the alternative hypothesis (H₁): There is a real difference or relationship.
Compute a test statistic that measures how far the observed data deviates from what the null hypothesis would predict.
Compute a p-value — the probability of seeing a test statistic this extreme (or more extreme) if the null hypothesis were true.
Interpret the result. A small p-value (conventionally below 0.05) means the observed pattern is unlikely to have arisen by chance alone. A large p-value means you do not have enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis.

A p-value does not tell you the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It tells you how surprising your data would be if the null hypothesis were true. This distinction matters: a p-value of 0.03 means “if there were truly no difference, we’d see data this extreme only 3% of the time” — not “there is a 97% chance the difference is real.”






WarningStatistical significance is not practical significance



A large dataset can produce a tiny p-value for a difference that is too small to matter. Always report and interpret the effect size — the magnitude of the difference — alongside the p-value. A statistically significant but tiny difference is not analytically useful.

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.4 Comparing Two Groups: The t-Test
The independent samples t-test asks: Is the mean of one group different from the mean of another group? Use it when you have one numeric outcome and two categorical groups.

17.4.1 Example: Batting average before and after the steroid era
The late 1990s through early 2000s are sometimes called baseball’s “steroid era.” Did batting averages differ between the pre-steroid period (1980–1993) and the steroid era (1994–2004)?

pre_steroid = qualified[
    (qualified['yearID'] >= 1980) & (qualified['yearID'] <= 1993)
]['BA'].dropna()

steroid_era = qualified[
    (qualified['yearID'] >= 1994) & (qualified['yearID'] <= 2004)
]['BA'].dropna()

print(f'Pre-steroid: n={len(pre_steroid)}, mean={pre_steroid.mean():.4f}, std={pre_steroid.std():.4f}')
print(f'Steroid era: n={len(steroid_era)}, mean={steroid_era.mean():.4f}, std={steroid_era.std():.4f}')

Visualize the distributions before testing:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.kdeplot(pre_steroid, label='Pre-steroid (1980–93)', fill=True, alpha=0.3, ax=ax)
sns.kdeplot(steroid_era, label='Steroid era (1994–2004)', fill=True, alpha=0.3, ax=ax)
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
ax.set_title('Batting Average Distribution: Pre-Steroid vs. Steroid Era')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Now run the t-test:

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(pre_steroid, steroid_era)

print(f't-statistic: {t_stat:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')



17.4.2 Interpreting the result
The mean difference between the two eras can be computed directly:

diff = steroid_era.mean() - pre_steroid.mean()
print(f'Mean difference: {diff:.4f}')

Here is what you can conclude: if the p-value is below 0.05, the difference in mean batting average between the two eras is statistically significant — unlikely to have arisen by chance. The effect size (the mean difference) tells you whether the difference is large enough to be practically meaningful. A difference of 0.005 in batting average is detectable with thousands of player-seasons but may not be substantively interesting. A difference of 0.015 would be more notable.
What you cannot conclude: that steroids caused the difference. These are different groups of players measured in different years with different rules, ballparks, and training methods. The comparison is between-subjects and observational — all the caveats from Chapter 14 apply.


17.4.3 Effect size: Cohen’s d
Cohen’s d measures the difference in means in terms of standard deviations. It provides a standardized measure of effect size that is comparable across different variables and studies.

pooled_std = np.sqrt((pre_steroid.std()**2 + steroid_era.std()**2) / 2)
cohens_d = (steroid_era.mean() - pre_steroid.mean()) / pooled_std

print(f"Cohen's d: {cohens_d:.3f}")

Rules of thumb for interpreting Cohen’s d: 0.2 is a small effect, 0.5 is medium, and 0.8 is large. These are guidelines, not thresholds — context determines what counts as meaningful in your domain.

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
section: 17.5 Comparing Two Related Measurements: The Paired t-Test
text:
17.5 Comparing Two Related Measurements: The Paired t-Test
A paired t-test is appropriate when you have two measurements from the same entity — for example, medical and retail cannabis sales from the same county in the same month. The question is: Is there a systematic difference between the two measurements?

# Compare medical vs retail sales for county-months where both exist
paired = cannabis[cannabis['medical'].notna() & cannabis['retail'].notna()].copy()

print(f'Paired observations: {len(paired)}')
print(f'Mean medical: ${paired["medical"].mean():,.0f}')
print(f'Mean retail:  ${paired["retail"].mean():,.0f}')

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_rel(paired['retail'], paired['medical'])

print(f'\nPaired t-test:')
print(f't-statistic: {t_stat:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.6f}')

Here is what you can conclude: if the p-value is very small, retail sales are systematically different from medical sales across county-months. The direction and magnitude of the difference tell you which is larger and by how much. This is a within-subjects comparison — the same counties are measured on both variables — so many county-level confounds (population, geography) are controlled for.

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
section: 17.6 Comparing Categorical Variables: The Chi-Square Test
text:
17.6 Comparing Categorical Variables: The Chi-Square Test
The chi-square test of independence asks: Are two categorical variables associated, or are they independent? Use it when both your variables are categorical.

17.6.1 Example: Has the distribution of home run tiers changed across eras?

# Bin HR into categories
hr_data = qualified[qualified['decade'].isin([1980, 2000])].copy()
hr_data['hr_tier'] = pd.cut(
    hr_data['HR'], bins=[-1, 5, 15, 30, 100],
    labels=['0-5', '6-15', '16-30', '31+']
)
hr_data['era'] = hr_data['decade'].map({1980: '1980s', 2000: '2000s'})

# Build contingency table
contingency = pd.crosstab(hr_data['era'], hr_data['hr_tier'])
print('Contingency table:')
print(contingency)


chi2, p_value, dof, expected = stats.chi2_contingency(contingency)

print(f'\nChi-square statistic: {chi2:.2f}')
print(f'Degrees of freedom: {dof}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.6f}')

Here is what you can conclude: if the p-value is small, the distribution of home run tiers differs significantly between the two eras — the two variables (era and HR tier) are not independent. To understand how they differ, compare the observed counts to the expected counts (what you would see if era and HR tier were independent):

expected_df = pd.DataFrame(
    expected, index=contingency.index, columns=contingency.columns
).round(1)

print('Expected counts (if independent):')
print(expected_df)
print('\nObserved - Expected:')
print((contingency - expected_df).round(1))

Positive residuals indicate cells with more observations than expected; negative residuals indicate fewer. This tells you which specific categories drive the overall association.


17.6.2 Effect size: Cramér’s V
Cramér’s V is the chi-square analog of Cohen’s d — a measure of how strongly the two categorical variables are associated, scaled from 0 (independent) to 1 (perfectly associated):

n = contingency.sum().sum()
min_dim = min(contingency.shape) - 1
cramers_v = np.sqrt(chi2 / (n * min_dim))

print(f"Cramér's V: {cramers_v:.3f}")

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.7 Measuring Linear Relationships: Correlation
The Pearson correlation coefficient (r) measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two numeric variables. It ranges from -1 (perfect negative linear relationship) to +1 (perfect positive linear relationship), with 0 indicating no linear relationship.

17.7.1 Example: Salary and performance

# Filter to a single recent season with enough data
season_2015 = bat_sal[bat_sal['yearID'] == 2015].dropna(subset=['salary', 'OPS'])

r, p_value = stats.pearsonr(season_2015['salary'], season_2015['OPS'])

print(f'Pearson r: {r:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')
print(f'n: {len(season_2015)}')

Visualize alongside the statistic:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(season_2015['salary'] / 1e6, season_2015['OPS'], alpha=0.4, s=20)
ax.set_xlabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_ylabel('OPS')
ax.set_title(f'Salary vs. OPS (2015) — r = {r:.3f}, p = {p_value:.4f}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Here is what you can conclude: the correlation coefficient tells you the strength and direction of the linear relationship. A positive r means higher salary is associated with higher OPS. The p-value tells you whether this correlation is statistically significant. The magnitude of r tells you how tightly the points cluster around a line — an r of 0.3 indicates a weak-to-moderate positive relationship, meaning salary explains about 9% (r² = 0.09) of the variation in OPS.
What you cannot conclude: that paying a player more would improve their performance. As discussed in Chapter 14, the causal arrow is ambiguous, and the relationship is confounded by age, experience, and roster construction.


17.7.2 Spearman correlation for non-linear monotonic relationships
Pearson’s r measures linear relationships. If the relationship is monotonic but curved (both variables increase together, but not in a straight line), Spearman’s rank correlation is more appropriate:

rho, p_value = stats.spearmanr(season_2015['salary'], season_2015['OPS'])

print(f'Spearman rho: {rho:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')

Spearman’s ρ (rho) works by ranking both variables and computing the Pearson correlation on the ranks. It is robust to outliers and does not assume the relationship is linear.

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.8 Correlation Across Many Variables
When you have several numeric columns, compute the full pairwise correlation matrix and identify which pairs have the strongest relationships:

metrics = bat_sal[['AB', 'HR', 'BB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS', 'salary']].dropna()
corr = metrics.corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', center=0,
            square=True, linewidths=0.5, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Correlation Matrix — Batting Metrics and Salary')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Look for the cells with the strongest colors. Correlations between derived metrics (like OBP and OPS) will be high by construction. The interesting correlations are between conceptually distinct variables — salary and performance metrics, counting stats and rate stats.

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.9 Summary: Choosing a Test








Question
Test
Input




Is the mean different between two independent groups?
Independent t-test (stats.ttest_ind)
One numeric + one binary categorical


Is there a systematic difference between two paired measurements?
Paired t-test (stats.ttest_rel)
Two numeric columns from the same entity


Are two categorical variables associated?
Chi-square test (stats.chi2_contingency)
Two categorical variables (contingency table)


How strong is the linear relationship between two variables?
Pearson correlation (stats.pearsonr)
Two numeric variables


How strong is the monotonic (but possibly non-linear) relationship?
Spearman correlation (stats.spearmanr)
Two numeric variables

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.10 Reporting Results
When reporting the result of a statistical test, include four elements:

What you tested: “We compared mean batting average between the pre-steroid (1980–93) and steroid (1994–2004) eras.”
The result: “The steroid era had a slightly higher mean batting average (0.271 vs. 0.267).”
The test statistic and p-value: “t(8452) = -3.21, p = 0.001”
The effect size and interpretation: “Cohen’s d = 0.07, a small effect. While the difference is statistically significant due to the large sample size, the practical difference of 4 points of batting average is modest.”

This pattern — what, result, statistics, interpretation — keeps your reporting honest and complete. It prevents the common mistake of reporting a p-value without context about the magnitude of the effect.

title: 17  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
17.11 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: t-test for home runs





Compare the mean number of home runs per qualified player-season between the 1980s and the 2010s. Run an independent t-test and compute Cohen’s d. Is the difference statistically significant? Is it practically meaningful?






NoteSolution






hr_80s = qualified[qualified['decade'] == 1980]['HR'].dropna()
hr_10s = qualified[qualified['decade'] == 2010]['HR'].dropna()

print(f'1980s: n={len(hr_80s)}, mean={hr_80s.mean():.2f}')
print(f'2010s: n={len(hr_10s)}, mean={hr_10s.mean():.2f}')

t, p = stats.ttest_ind(hr_80s, hr_10s)
pooled = np.sqrt((hr_80s.std()**2 + hr_10s.std()**2) / 2)
d = (hr_10s.mean() - hr_80s.mean()) / pooled

print(f'\nt = {t:.3f}, p = {p:.4f}')
print(f"Cohen's d = {d:.3f}")













NoteExercise 2: Chi-square for cannabis counties





Create a contingency table of Colorado counties by year (pick two years at least 5 years apart) and whether they reported any retail sales (yes/no). Run a chi-square test. Has the proportion of counties with retail sales changed significantly?






NoteSolution






for_chi = cannabis[cannabis['year'].isin([2016, 2023])].copy()
for_chi['has_retail'] = for_chi['retail'].notna() & (for_chi['retail'] > 0)
yearly = for_chi.groupby(['year', 'county'])['has_retail'].any().reset_index()

ct = pd.crosstab(yearly['year'], yearly['has_retail'])
ct.columns = ['No Retail', 'Has Retail']
print(ct)

chi2, p, dof, exp = stats.chi2_contingency(ct)
print(f'\nChi-square = {chi2:.2f}, p = {p:.4f}')













NoteExercise 3: Correlation exploration





Compute the Pearson and Spearman correlations between at-bats (AB) and salary for the 2010s. Which is larger, and what does the difference tell you about the shape of the relationship? Produce a scatter plot to visually confirm your interpretation.






NoteSolution






recent = bat_sal[bat_sal['decade'] == 2010].dropna(subset=['AB', 'salary'])

r_pearson, p_pearson = stats.pearsonr(recent['AB'], recent['salary'])
r_spearman, p_spearman = stats.spearmanr(recent['AB'], recent['salary'])

print(f'Pearson r = {r_pearson:.3f} (p = {p_pearson:.4f})')
print(f'Spearman ρ = {r_spearman:.3f} (p = {p_spearman:.4f})')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(recent['AB'], recent['salary']/1e6, alpha=0.3, s=15)
ax.set_xlabel('At Bats')
ax.set_ylabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_title(f'AB vs. Salary — Pearson r={r_pearson:.3f}, Spearman ρ={r_spearman:.3f}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

If Spearman is larger than Pearson, the relationship is monotonic but nonlinear — likely because salary increases steeply at high AB counts but flattens at low counts.

Known quirks:

Delimiter is | (pipe), not comma. Use sep='|' in read_csv.
VOTER_ID, RES_ZIP_CODE, BALLOT_ZIP, and PRECINCT contain leading zeros and should be loaded as strings using the dtype parameter.
Party affiliation codes are inconsistent — multiple abbreviations for the same party (e.g., DEM, Dem, D, Democrat).
Some text fields contain extra whitespace, non-breaking spaces, or inconsistent capitalization.

text:

Used in: Chapter 8, Chapter 9
A comprehensive historical database of Major League Baseball statistics. The book uses four tables from this database.

A.3.1 Batting.csv
Each row represents one player’s statistics for one team in one season. Players traded mid-season have multiple rows (one per “stint”).

HBP and SF are NULL for early seasons when these statistics were not recorded. Filling with 0 is a common choice but should be documented.

title: Appendix A — Datasets
section: A.4 Colorado State Demography Office — Single Year of Age Estimates
text:
A.4 Colorado State Demography Office — Single Year of Age Estimates
Source: Colorado Department of Local Affairs (DOLA), State Demography Office
URL: https://demography.dola.colorado.gov/assets/html/sdodata.html
File: sya-county.csv
Used in: Chapter 8, Chapter 9, Chapter 11, Chapter 13
Population estimates by county, year, and single year of age (0 through 100). Includes both historical estimates and future forecasts.



Column
Type
Description




countyfips
text
FIPS county code (with leading zeros)


county
text
County name


year
integer
Calendar year


age
integer
Single year of age (0–100)


malepopulation
integer
Male population count


femalepopulation
integer
Female population count


totalpopulation
integer
Total population count


datatype
text
Estimate (historical) or Forecast (projected)



Known quirks:

The first row of the CSV is a metadata header. Use skiprows=1 in read_csv.
countyfips has leading zeros and should be loaded as a string.
The datatype column distinguishes historical estimates from projections. Filter to Estimate rows for historical analysis.
County names use bare names (e.g., Adams, not Adams County). This format differs from the CDC and CBI datasets and must be standardized before joining.
Contains a DtypeWarning on some columns due to mixed types.

Known quirks:

County names are in the format 'Adams County, CO'. This must be standardized to match other datasets.
Death counts below 10 may be suppressed for privacy. The value appears as Suppressed rather than a number.
